overview.pathophysiology: Age-related syndrome of decreased physiological reserve and resilience. Characterized by muscle loss (sarcopenia), reduced strength, slow gait, low activity, exhaustion. Increases vulnerability to stressors and adverse outcomes.
overview.functional_impact: Reduced functional independence, high falls risk, institutionalization risk, poor surgical outcomes, increased mortality, disability, hospitalization.
overview.prevalence: Affects 10-15% of adults >65 years, increasing to 25-50% in those >85 years. Pre-frailty affects additional 35-50%.

assessment.evidence_base: Comprehensive geriatric assessment including frailty screening per Australian Frailty Network guidelines

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 8-12 reps at 60-80% 1RM, 2-3x/week, major muscle groups
exercises.purpose: Reverse sarcopenia, improve strength, functional capacity - KEY INTERVENTION
exercises.modifications: Seated exercises if standing unsafe. Start low resistance. Include power training (fast movements).
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - most critical component

exercises.name: Balance Training
exercises.purpose: Reduce falls risk, improve stability, confidence
exercises.modifications: Multi-directional, narrow base, dual-task, perturbations
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence for falls reduction

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Improve cardiovascular fitness, endurance
exercises.modifications: Walking primary modality. Seated options if needed. Interval approach if continuous difficult.

exercises.name: Flexibility Exercises
exercises.purpose: Maintain mobility, prevent contractures
exercises.modifications: Focus on major joints, gentle sustained stretches
exercises.evidence_level: Low quality evidence alone, important as adjunct

exercises.name: Functional Task Training
exercises.purpose: Improve ADL capacity, transfers, gait
exercises.modifications: Sit-to-stand practice, stair climbing, reaching tasks

exercise_prescription.frequency: 3-5 sessions per week - frequent exposure important
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 12 weeks to show significant benefits. Ongoing long-term exercise essential.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Multicomponent exercise (strength + balance + aerobic) reverses frailty in 15-50% of cases. Improves physical function, reduces disability, hospitalizations, and mortality. Resistance training most critical component - reverses sarcopenia. Higher intensity more effective than low intensity when tolerated. Nutritional intervention enhances exercise effects (protein supplementation).

phases.goals: Comprehensive assessment, establish safe baseline, address medical issues
phases.criteria: Screen for falls risk, malnutrition, polypharmacy, cognitive impairment. Medical clearance.

phases.goals: Build basic strength, balance, tolerance to exercise
phases.criteria: Supervised exercise 2-3x/week. Low-moderate intensity. Progress when tolerating well.

phases.goals: Increase intensity and volume, optimize benefits
phases.criteria: Progress resistance and challenge. Increase to 3+ sessions/week. Functional improvements expected.

phases.goals: Maintain/build on gains, prevent decline
phases.criteria: Continued minimum 2-3x/week. Community programs. Periodic reassessments.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach with close supervision given vulnerability. Minimum 12 weeks required per research evidence.

relative: Severe cognitive impairment (may need adapted programs)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvements in strength and function within 6-8 weeks. Frailty status may improve after 12+ weeks.
key_outcomes: Reversal of frailty (15-50% depending on severity)
Improved SPPB score (1+ point clinically meaningful)
success_indicators: Transition from frail to pre-frail or robust
outcomes.effect_sizes: Physical function: SMD 0.46 (95% CI 0.29-0.64). Mobility: SMD 0.40. ADLs: SMD 0.28. Mortality: RR 0.90 (0.85-0.99).

key_findings: Multicomponent exercise improves physical function in frail older adults
Resistance training most effective component
Can reverse frailty in significant proportion
Reduces disability and improves ADL independence
May reduce mortality and hospitalizations
Protein supplementation enhances effects
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: SPPB: MD 1.06 points (0.61-1.50). Gait speed: MD 0.08 m/s (0.05-0.11). Frailty prevalence: RR 0.55 (0.38-0.79). Mortality: RR 0.90 (0.85-0.99).
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence from numerous RCTs including large trials

references.citation: Apóstolo J, et al. Effectiveness of interventions to prevent pre-frailty and frailty progression in older adults. JBI Database System Rev Implement Rep. 2018
references.key_finding: Exercise can prevent and reverse frailty in older adults

references.citation: Cadore EL, et al. Effects of different exercise interventions on risk of falls, gait ability, and balance in physically frail older adults. Rejuvenation Res. 2013
references.key_finding: Multicomponent training most effective for frail older adults

references.citation: Australian Frailty Network. Frailty Management Guidelines. 2020
references.key_finding: Australian evidence-based approach emphasizing exercise and nutrition

clinical_note: Multidisciplinary approach essential - GP, geriatrician, EP, PT, OT, dietitian, pharmacist. Address polypharmacy (>5 medications increases frailty). Nutritional assessment and protein supplementation critical (1.2-1.5g/kg/day). Screen for depression, social isolation. Home safety assessment. Comprehensive geriatric assessment. Close supervision and safety monitoring essential given vulnerability.

overview.pathophysiology: Systemic skeletal disease characterized by low bone mass and microarchitectural deterioration, increasing fracture risk. Results from imbalance between bone resorption and formation.
overview.functional_impact: Often asymptomatic until fracture. Fractures (hip, spine, wrist) cause pain, disability, reduced independence, increased mortality (especially hip fractures).
overview.prevalence: Affects ~20% of women and 5% of men >50 years in Australia. Prevalence increases with age. Major public health issue.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per Osteoporosis Australia and Royal Australian College of GPs guidelines

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Increase/maintain BMD, improve muscle strength, reduce falls - KEY INTERVENTION
exercises.modifications: Higher loads more effective for BMD. Major muscle groups.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence for BMD, strength, falls

exercises.name: Weight-Bearing Impact Exercise
exercises.purpose: Osteogenic stimulus - most potent for BMD
exercises.modifications: Jumping, hopping, skipping. Start low, progress gradually. Not for very high fracture risk.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - most effective for BMD

exercises.name: Balance Training
exercises.purpose: Reduce falls risk - critical for fracture prevention
exercises.modifications: Progressive challenge, multi-directional, dual-task
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence for falls reduction

exercises.name: Postural Exercises
exercises.purpose: Reduce kyphosis, improve posture, reduce vertebral fracture risk
exercises.modifications: Prone back extensions, rowing exercises

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Ongoing lifelong intervention - benefits require sustained participation
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Progressive resistance training + impact exercise most effective for BMD (1-3% increase possible at spine/hip). Multicomponent programs (resistance + balance + impact) reduce fractures by preventing falls. High loads and impacts more effective than low intensity. Exercise alone modest effect on BMD but significant fracture reduction through falls prevention and muscle strengthening.

phases.goals: Build strength and balance safely, establish habit
phases.criteria: Supervised initially. Moderate loads. Low-impact initially if very high risk.

phases.goals: Increase loads and impact progressively
phases.criteria: Progress toward 70-80% 1RM resistance. Add impact if appropriate.

phases.goals: Maintain BMD, prevent falls and fractures
phases.criteria: Sustained exercise 3-5x/week. Continued challenge and progression.

progression.evidence_base: Long-term sustained exercise required for fracture prevention

absolute: Severe osteoporosis with recent fragility fracture (requires modified approach)
relative: Very high fracture risk (modify - avoid high impact/spinal flexion)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: BMD changes take 6-12 months to detect. Falls reduction within 3-6 months.
success_indicators: Stable or increased BMD on DEXA
outcomes.effect_sizes: BMD: +1-3% spine, +0.5-2% hip with high-intensity programs. Falls: RR 0.77. Fractures: RR 0.84 (exercise alone), 0.66 (multicomponent).

key_findings: Progressive resistance training increases BMD
High-impact exercise most effective for BMD
Multicomponent programs reduce fractures
Falls reduction major mechanism for fracture prevention
Higher intensity more effective than low
Benefits require sustained long-term participation
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: BMD spine: +1-2% with resistance/impact. BMD hip: +0.5-1.5%. Falls: RR 0.77 (0.71-0.83). Fractures: RR 0.84 (0.72-0.97).
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and meta-analyses

references.citation: Howe TE, et al. Exercise for preventing and treating osteoporosis in postmenopausal women. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2011
references.key_finding: Exercise increases BMD and reduces fracture risk

references.citation: Osteoporosis Australia. Exercise Recommendations. 2020
references.key_finding: Australian evidence-based exercise guidelines for osteoporosis

references.citation: Beck BR, et al. Exercise and Sports Science Australia position statement on exercise prescription for the prevention and management of osteoporosis. J Sci Med Sport. 2017
references.key_finding: High-intensity progressive resistance and impact training recommended

clinical_note: Avoid spinal flexion exercises (increase vertebral fracture risk). High loads and impacts most effective BUT must assess individual fracture risk. Very high risk may need modified program. Calcium (1000-1300 mg/day) and vitamin D (800-2000 IU/day) essential adjuncts. Address falls risk comprehensively. Screen for secondary causes. Medications (bisphosphonates, etc.) may be needed in addition to exercise. Multidisciplinary care with GP/endocrinologist.

overview.pathophysiology: Atherosclerotic narrowing of peripheral arteries, most commonly lower limbs. Reduced blood flow causes claudication (exercise-induced leg pain) and tissue ischemia. Marker of systemic atherosclerosis.
overview.functional_impact: Intermittent claudication limiting walking distance, leg fatigue/pain, reduced functional capacity, impaired quality of life. Associated cardiovascular mortality risk.
overview.prevalence: Affects 3-5% of adults >50 years, increasing to 15-20% in those >70. Higher in smokers, diabetics. Often undiagnosed.

key_assessments: Claudication onset and peak walking distances
assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per Vascular Society guidelines and TASC recommendations

exercises.name: Supervised Walking Exercise
exercises.dosage: Walk to moderate-severe claudication pain, rest until pain resolves, repeat. 30-60 min sessions, 3x/week minimum
exercises.purpose: Improve walking distance, collateral circulation, mitochondrial function - GOLD STANDARD
exercises.modifications: Treadmill or track walking. Supervised initially.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - most effective intervention

exercises.name: Alternative Modes (if walking contraindicated)
exercises.purpose: Alternative if walking not tolerated
exercises.modifications: Useful for those with joint problems
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence - less effective than walking

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Improve muscle strength, complement walking
exercises.modifications: Include lower limb exercises

exercises.name: Home Walking Program
exercises.purpose: Supplement supervised sessions, promote independence
exercises.modifications: Walk to claudication, rest, repeat
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence - less effective than supervised

exercise_prescription.frequency: Supervised walking 3x/week minimum, daily home walking ideal
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 12 weeks supervised, ongoing long-term
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Supervised exercise therapy as effective as angioplasty/stenting for claudication. Improves walking distance by 50-200% over 12 weeks. Walking to moderate-severe pain essential - 'no pain no gain'. Supervised programs superior to unsupervised. Benefits maintained with continued exercise. Reduces cardiovascular events and mortality.

phases.goals: Establish tolerance, improve walking distance, education
phases.criteria: 3x/week supervised. Walk to claudication, rest, repeat. Gradual progression of duration.

phases.goals: Sustain improvements, prevent decline
phases.criteria: Continue home walking program. Periodic supervised sessions. Ongoing cardiovascular risk management.

progression.evidence_base: Minimum 12 weeks supervised exercise per international guidelines

absolute: Critical limb ischemia with rest pain/tissue loss

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Significant improvements in walking distance within 8-12 weeks
outcomes.effect_sizes: Maximal walking distance: +180m (95% CI 130-230m). Pain-free walking distance: +90m (60-120m). Large effect sizes vs. control.

key_findings: Supervised exercise most effective treatment for claudication
As effective as angioplasty/stenting without risks
Improves walking distance by 50-200%
Supervised superior to unsupervised
Walking to moderate-severe pain essential
Reduces cardiovascular mortality
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Maximal walking distance: MD +180m (130-230m). Exercise vs. angioplasty: no significant difference. Exercise + angioplasty superior to angioplasty alone.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and meta-analyses

references.citation: Lane R, et al. Exercise for intermittent claudication. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2017
references.key_finding: Exercise therapy highly effective for improving walking distance in claudication

references.citation: Fakhry F, et al. Supervised walking therapy in patients with intermittent claudication. J Vasc Surg. 2012
references.key_finding: Supervised exercise as effective as angioplasty for claudication

references.citation: TASC II Guidelines. Eur J Vasc Endovasc Surg. 2007
references.key_finding: Exercise therapy recommended as first-line treatment for claudication

clinical_note: Supervised programs essential - home programs alone less effective. Walking to moderate-severe pain necessary ('no pain no gain'). Cardiovascular risk factor management critical (smoking cessation ESSENTIAL, BP control, lipids, diabetes, antiplatelet therapy). Coordinate with vascular specialist. ABI monitoring. Screen for AAA. Most can avoid or delay revascularization with exercise. Exercise reduces overall cardiovascular mortality.

overview.pathophysiology: Chronic autoimmune inflammatory disease affecting synovial joints. Characterized by synovitis, joint destruction, systemic inflammation. Progressive joint damage if untreated.
overview.functional_impact: Joint pain, stiffness (especially morning), swelling, reduced ROM, muscle weakness, fatigue, reduced functional capacity, disability.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~2% of Australian adults. More common in females (3:1 ratio). Peak onset 40-60 years.

screening_tools: Red flag screening for active flare
assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per EULAR and Australian Rheumatology Association guidelines

exercises.name: Range of Motion Exercises
exercises.purpose: Maintain joint mobility, prevent contractures
exercises.modifications: Gentle, within pain limits, morning routine important

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Improve strength, function, bone density
exercises.modifications: Avoid during acute flares, modify for joint involvement
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - safe and effective

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Improve cardiovascular fitness, reduce fatigue, mood
exercises.modifications: Low-impact (cycling, swimming, water exercise) preferred

exercises.name: Hand Exercises
exercises.purpose: Maintain hand function, grip strength
exercises.modifications: Therapeutic putty, grip exercises, dexterity tasks

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Ongoing lifelong intervention
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise safe and beneficial in RA. Does not increase disease activity or joint damage. Improves physical function, cardiovascular fitness, and reduces fatigue. High-intensity training safe when disease controlled. Water-based exercise well-tolerated during flares.

phases.goals: Maintain ROM, prevent deconditioning
phases.criteria: Gentle ROM exercises, rest as needed. Reduce intensity, not frequency.

phases.goals: Build fitness, strength, function
phases.criteria: Progressive exercise program. Monitor for flare signs.

phases.goals: Maintain fitness, manage symptoms, prevent disability
phases.criteria: Continued regular exercise, adjust for disease activity.

progression.evidence_base: Exercise safe in stable and active RA per EULAR recommendations

absolute: Severe acute flare with systemic symptoms

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvements in function and fitness within 8-12 weeks
outcomes.effect_sizes: Physical function: SMD 0.3-0.4. Aerobic capacity: 10-20% increase. Strength: 20-40% increase. No increase in disease activity.

key_findings: Exercise safe in RA, does not increase disease activity
Improves physical function and cardiovascular fitness
High-intensity exercise safe when disease controlled
Reduces fatigue and improves QoL
No detrimental effects on joints
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Physical function: SMD 0.30 (0.13-0.47). Aerobic capacity: SMD 1.04 (0.48-1.60). No significant effect on disease activity.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence from RCTs

references.citation: Hurkmans E, et al. Dynamic exercise programs for rheumatoid arthritis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2009
references.key_finding: Exercise therapy safe and beneficial for RA patients

references.citation: EULAR recommendations for physical activity in inflammatory arthritis. Ann Rheum Dis. 2018
references.key_finding: Evidence-based exercise recommendations for RA

clinical_note: Coordinate with rheumatologist. Medication timing may affect exercise tolerance. Monitor for flares. Educate on exercise safety. Address cardiovascular risk (increased in RA). Refer to OT for joint protection strategies. Multidisciplinary approach essential.

overview.pathophysiology: Degenerative joint disease of the hip involving cartilage breakdown, bone changes, synovial inflammation. Progressive loss of joint structure and function.
overview.functional_impact: Hip/groin pain, stiffness, reduced ROM, altered gait, difficulty with stairs/ADLs, reduced walking tolerance.
overview.prevalence: Affects 8-10% of adults >45 years. Increases with age. Major cause of disability and joint replacement.

key_assessments: Hip ROM (flexion, internal rotation particularly limited)
outcome_measures: Hip Disability and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (HOOS)
assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per OARSI and Australian hip OA guidelines

exercises.name: Hip Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Improve hip muscle strength, reduce pain, enhance function
exercises.modifications: Include hip abductors, extensors, flexors. Progress resistance.

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Improve fitness, weight management, reduce inflammation
exercises.modifications: Low-impact: cycling, swimming preferred over walking

exercises.name: Balance Training
exercises.purpose: Improve stability, reduce falls risk
exercises.modifications: Single-leg stance, perturbations

exercises.name: Flexibility Exercises
exercises.purpose: Maintain hip ROM
exercises.modifications: Hip flexors, adductors particularly important

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 8-12 weeks, ongoing recommended
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise reduces pain and improves function in hip OA with moderate effect sizes. Land-based strengthening particularly effective. Aquatic exercise beneficial for those with severe symptoms. Delays need for joint replacement.

phases.goals: Reduce pain, improve basic strength
phases.criteria: Low resistance, technique focus. Progress when pain controlled.

phases.goals: Build strength and function
phases.criteria: Increase resistance and complexity.

phases.goals: Maintain gains, delay progression
phases.criteria: Continued 2-3x/week minimum.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach per best practice guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvements within 6-8 weeks
outcomes.effect_sizes: Pain: SMD -0.38. Function: SMD -0.38. Similar to NSAIDs.

key_findings: Exercise reduces pain and improves function in hip OA
Strengthening most effective
Similar effects to knee OA exercise
Weight loss enhances outcomes if overweight
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate quality evidence from RCTs

references.citation: Fransen M, et al. Exercise for osteoarthritis of the hip. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014
references.key_finding: Land-based exercise reduces pain and improves function in hip OA

references.citation: OARSI guidelines for hip OA. Osteoarthritis Cartilage. 2019
references.key_finding: Exercise strongly recommended for hip OA

clinical_note: Education critical - address misconceptions. Weight loss if overweight (refer to APD). Consider referral for joint replacement if conservative management fails.

overview.pathophysiology: Residual symptoms following acute lateral ankle sprain. Involves ligament laxity, proprioceptive deficits, muscle weakness, altered neuromuscular control.
overview.functional_impact: Recurrent sprains, giving way, ankle instability, reduced sports/activity participation, chronic pain, early ankle OA risk.
overview.prevalence: 40-50% develop chronic instability after acute sprain. Common in athletes.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per International Ankle Consortium guidelines

exercises.name: Balance and Proprioception Training
exercises.purpose: Restore proprioception, dynamic stability - KEY INTERVENTION
exercises.modifications: Progress from two-leg to single-leg, eyes closed, unstable surfaces, perturbations
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - reduces reinjury by 35-50%

exercises.name: Ankle Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Improve ankle muscle strength, dynamic stability
exercises.modifications: Include dorsiflexion, plantarflexion, inversion, eversion. Progress resistance.

exercises.name: Functional Training
exercises.purpose: Return to sport, functional activities
exercises.modifications: Include cutting, jumping, landing tasks. Progress complexity.

exercises.name: Flexibility
exercises.purpose: Maintain ankle ROM
exercises.modifications: Gastrocnemius, soleus stretching

exercise_prescription.frequency: 5-7 days per week for balance, 3x/week for strengthening
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 12 weeks, ongoing for high-risk activities
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Neuromuscular training reduces recurrent sprains by 35-50%. Balance training most critical component. Supervised programs more effective than home-only. Early intervention prevents chronic instability development.

phases.goals: Restore basic balance, strength, ROM
phases.criteria: Basic single-leg balance, pain-free ROM.

phases.goals: Progress balance difficulty, build strength
phases.criteria: Unstable surface balance, increased resistance.

phases.goals: Sport-specific training, reinjury prevention
phases.criteria: Pass return to sport tests.

phases.goals: Prevent reinjury
phases.criteria: Continued balance training 2-3x/week.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive neuromuscular training per best evidence

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improved stability within 6-8 weeks. Reduced reinjury over 12+ months.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Reinjury prevention: RR 0.65 (0.50-0.83) with balance training.

key_findings: Balance training reduces recurrent sprains by 35-50%
Effective for both acute and chronic instability
Supervised better than unsupervised
Ankle strengthening enhances outcomes
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Reinjury: RR 0.65 (95% CI 0.50-0.83). Number needed to treat: 7.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from RCTs

references.citation: Hiller CE, et al. Balance training for chronic ankle instability. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2011
references.key_finding: Balance training reduces recurrent ankle sprains

references.citation: International Ankle Consortium guidelines. Br J Sports Med. 2020
references.key_finding: Evidence-based recommendations for ankle instability management

clinical_note: Address footwear. Consider bracing for high-risk sports initially. Rule out other pathology if not improving. Emphasize prevention - ongoing balance training reduces reinjury.

overview.pathophysiology: Complex multisystem disorder characterized by profound fatigue not improved by rest. Pathophysiology unclear - involves immune, neurological, and metabolic dysregulation. Post-exertional malaise key feature.
overview.functional_impact: Severe fatigue, post-exertional malaise, cognitive impairment, sleep disturbance, pain, reduced functional capacity, disability, inability to work/study.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~0.2-0.4% of Australians. More common in females. Often follows viral illness.

key_assessments: Fukuda criteria or ICC criteria confirmation
assessment.evidence_base: Diagnosis per ICC or Fukuda criteria. Assessment per ME/CFS clinical guidelines

exercises.name: Graded Activity Management (Pacing)
exercises.dosage: Individualized to avoid post-exertional malaise, within energy envelope
exercises.purpose: Manage symptoms, prevent crashes, maintain function
exercises.modifications: Stop well before fatigue. Rest periods. Track energy levels.
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence - most widely recommended

exercises.name: Very Gentle Movement
exercises.dosage: 5-10 minutes very light activity, within tolerance
exercises.purpose: Prevent complete deconditioning, maintain mobility
exercises.modifications: Gentle stretching, slow walking, seated exercise. Must not trigger PEM.

exercises.name: Isometric Exercises
exercises.purpose: Maintain muscle without triggering PEM
exercises.modifications: Stop immediately if symptoms worsen

exercise_prescription.frequency: Variable based on individual tolerance and symptom severity
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Ongoing - focus on symptom management not fitness improvement
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: CRITICAL: Traditional exercise/graded exercise therapy (GET) can worsen symptoms and cause harm in CFS/ME. Pacing and staying within energy envelope recommended. Exercise must be highly individualized and stop well before triggering post-exertional malaise. Focus is symptom management, not fitness improvement. Many patients cannot tolerate traditional exercise programs.

phases.goals: Identify energy envelope, baseline tolerance
phases.criteria: Track activities and symptoms. Establish safe baseline that doesn't trigger PEM.

phases.goals: Maintain function within energy envelope, prevent crashes
phases.criteria: Consistent activity within limits. No progression if causes PEM.

phases.goals: Minimal increases only if consistently tolerated
phases.criteria: Increase by <10% only if no PEM for weeks. Regression common and expected.

progression.evidence_base: Pacing approach recommended by patient organizations and emerging clinical guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Symptom management focus - improvement unpredictable and highly variable
key_outcomes: Improved quality of life within constraints
outcomes.effect_sizes: Highly variable. Traditional GET shows harm in many patients. Pacing shows modest benefits in symptom management.

key_findings: Traditional GET can cause harm in CFS/ME patients
Pacing within energy envelope most recommended
Exercise must be highly individualized
Post-exertional malaise must be avoided
Focus on symptom management not fitness
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Limited pooled data. GET shows potential for harm. Pacing shows modest symptom improvement in some.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Low to moderate quality. Controversies exist. Patient harm reports significant for GET.

references.citation: NICE Guideline. Myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome: diagnosis and management. NG206. 2021
references.key_finding: Do not offer graded exercise therapy to people with ME/CFS. Recommend energy management.

references.citation: Geraghty K, et al. Myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome patients' reports of symptom changes following cognitive behavioural therapy, graded exercise therapy and pacing treatments. J Health Psychol. 2019
references.key_finding: Patients report high rates of deterioration with GET

clinical_note: CRITICAL: Approach with caution. Traditional exercise programs can cause significant harm. Listen to patient reports of PEM. Pacing within energy envelope recommended. Avoid pushing through fatigue. Rule out other treatable conditions. Multidisciplinary care essential. Refer to specialist CFS/ME services. Validate patient experience - this is a serious physical illness, not deconditioning.

overview.pathophysiology: Chronic elevation of blood pressure ≥140/90 mmHg. Multifactorial involving increased peripheral resistance, endothelial dysfunction, sympathetic activation, sodium retention.
overview.functional_impact: Often asymptomatic until complications. Increases risk of stroke, heart disease, kidney disease, cognitive decline. May cause headaches, dizziness.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~30% of Australian adults. Increases with age. Leading modifiable risk factor for cardiovascular disease.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per Australian Hypertension Guidelines and ACSM

exercises.name: Moderate Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Reduce BP, improve cardiovascular health - PRIMARY INTERVENTION
exercises.modifications: Walking most studied. Cycling, swimming also effective.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - reduces SBP by 5-8 mmHg

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Complement aerobic exercise, additional BP reduction
exercises.modifications: Circuit training format effective. Avoid valsalva maneuver.
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate to high quality evidence - reduces SBP by 3-4 mmHg

exercises.name: Isometric Exercise
exercises.dosage: 4x2 minutes at 30-40% MVC with 3 min rest, 3x/week
exercises.purpose: Significant BP reduction
exercises.modifications: Handgrip or wall squat. Emerging evidence.
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence - may reduce SBP by 5-10 mmHg

exercises.name: Dynamic Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: BP reduction with lower valsalva risk
exercises.modifications: Circuit training format

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Ongoing lifelong intervention
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise reduces BP by 5-8 mmHg (aerobic) and 3-4 mmHg (resistance). Effects similar to single antihypertensive medication. Benefits maintained with continued exercise. Higher volumes may provide greater reductions. Combination of aerobic and resistance training recommended.

phases.goals: Establish exercise habit, monitor BP response
phases.criteria: Start moderate intensity. Monitor BP pre/post exercise initially.

phases.goals: Progress to recommended volumes, optimize BP reduction
phases.criteria: Increase toward 150-300 min/week aerobic. Add resistance training.

phases.goals: Maintain exercise levels and BP control
phases.criteria: Sustained activity levels. Regular BP monitoring.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach per ACSM exercise and hypertension guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: BP reductions typically seen within 4-8 weeks
key_outcomes: Reduced resting BP (5-8 mmHg SBP, 3-5 mmHg DBP)
outcomes.effect_sizes: Aerobic: SBP -5 to -8 mmHg, DBP -3 to -5 mmHg. Resistance: SBP -3 to -4 mmHg. Effects additive with medications.

key_findings: Aerobic exercise reduces BP in hypertensive adults
Resistance training provides additional BP reduction
Effects independent of weight loss
Higher volumes associated with greater reductions
Benefits similar to single antihypertensive drug
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and meta-analyses

references.citation: Cornelissen VA, Smart NA. Exercise training for blood pressure: a systematic review and meta-analysis. J Am Heart Assoc. 2013
references.key_finding: Exercise training reduces BP in hypertensive individuals

references.citation: Whelton SP, et al. Effect of aerobic exercise on blood pressure: a meta-analysis. Ann Intern Med. 2002
references.key_finding: Aerobic exercise reduces BP by ~5-8 mmHg

references.citation: Australian Hypertension Guidelines. National Heart Foundation. 2016
references.key_finding: Exercise recommended as first-line therapy for hypertension

clinical_note: Coordinate with GP - medication adjustments may be needed as exercise lowers BP. Monitor BP regularly. Educate on home BP monitoring. Address other risk factors (diet - refer to APD, smoking, alcohol). Most benefit in those with elevated BP (prehypertension/stage 1). Exercise may prevent progression to medication need.

overview.pathophysiology: Chronic disease characterized by excess body fat accumulation. Results from energy imbalance. Associated with metabolic, hormonal, inflammatory, and genetic factors. Increases risk of diabetes, cardiovascular disease, cancer, osteoarthritis.
overview.functional_impact: Reduced physical function, joint pain, reduced mobility, breathlessness, fatigue, reduced quality of life, psychosocial impacts, increased chronic disease risk.
overview.prevalence: ~67% of Australian adults overweight or obese. Rising prevalence in children. Major public health concern.

assessment.evidence_base: Comprehensive assessment per Australian Obesity Management Algorithm and ACSM guidelines

exercises.name: Moderate-Intensity Aerobic Exercise
exercises.dosage: 200-300 minutes/week for weight loss, 150-250 min/week for prevention
exercises.purpose: Energy expenditure, fat oxidation, cardiovascular health, weight management
exercises.modifications: Low-impact options (walking, cycling, swimming) better tolerated. Progress duration before intensity.

exercises.name: High-Intensity Interval Training (HIIT)
exercises.purpose: Efficient calorie expenditure, metabolic benefits, time-efficient
exercises.modifications: Only when baseline fitness adequate. Sprint interval training (SIT) alternative.
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence - comparable to continuous for weight loss

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 8-12 reps, 2-3x/week, major muscle groups
exercises.purpose: Preserve/increase lean mass during weight loss, increase RMR, metabolic health
exercises.modifications: Critical to prevent lean mass loss. Progress resistance regularly.

exercises.name: Incidental Activity and NEAT
exercises.purpose: Increase total energy expenditure, sustainable behavior change
exercises.modifications: Active transport, standing desk, frequent movement breaks, step goals

exercise_prescription.session_duration: 45-60 minutes per session (or accumulated throughout day)
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 6 months for clinically significant weight loss. Ongoing lifelong for maintenance.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise alone produces modest weight loss (2-3kg) but critical for preventing weight regain and preserving lean mass. Combined with dietary intervention produces 10-15% weight loss. Higher volumes (250-300 min/week) required for weight loss vs. prevention (150 min/week). Resistance training essential to preserve lean mass. Exercise improves cardiometabolic health independent of weight loss.

phases.goals: Establish exercise habit, build tolerance, gradual progression
phases.criteria: Start low (e.g., 10-15 min sessions), focus on consistency and enjoyment. Progress when tolerating well.

phases.goals: Progress toward recommended volumes, optimize weight loss
phases.criteria: Gradually increase to 250-300 min/week aerobic. Add/progress resistance training. Weight loss 0.5-1kg/week.

phases.goals: Prevent weight regain, maintain health benefits
phases.criteria: Minimum 250-300 min/week to prevent regain. Ongoing resistance training. Dietary adherence. Regular monitoring.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach with emphasis on long-term sustainability. Weight maintenance requires ongoing high volumes of activity.

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Gradual weight loss of 0.5-1% body weight per week. Fitness improvements within 4-8 weeks.
success_indicators: Improved health markers independent of weight
outcomes.effect_sizes: Exercise alone: -2.1kg (95% CI -2.8 to -1.4). Exercise + diet: -11kg vs. -8kg diet alone. Weight regain prevention: exercise highly effective.

key_findings: Exercise alone produces modest weight loss (~2-3kg)
Exercise + diet superior to diet alone
Higher volumes (250-300 min/week) needed for weight loss
Resistance training preserves lean mass
Exercise highly effective for preventing weight regain
Health benefits independent of weight loss
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Weight loss from exercise alone: -2.1kg (-2.8 to -1.4). Exercise + diet vs diet alone: additional -3kg. Weight regain prevention: exercise critical component.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and long-term studies

references.citation: Donnelly JE, et al. ACSM position stand on appropriate physical activity intervention strategies for weight loss and prevention of weight regain. Med Sci Sports Exerc. 2009;41:459-71
references.key_finding: Comprehensive evidence-based recommendations for exercise in weight management

references.citation: Swift DL, et al. The role of exercise and physical activity in weight loss and maintenance. Prog Cardiovasc Dis. 2014;56:441-7
references.key_finding: Exercise essential for weight maintenance, modest for weight loss

references.citation: Clinical Practice Guidelines for Weight Management in Australia. NHMRC. 2013
references.key_finding: Australian guidelines emphasizing multicomponent approach

clinical_note: Emphasize health benefits independent of weight loss to maintain motivation. Address weight bias and stigma. Focus on sustainable behavior change. Dietary intervention essential - refer to APD. Screen for binge eating disorder, depression. Consider medication/surgery in appropriate cases (with medical team). Multidisciplinary approach critical. Set realistic expectations - 5-10% weight loss is clinically meaningful.

overview.pathophysiology: Mental health condition characterized by persistent excessive worry and anxiety. Involves dysregulation of fear circuitry, amygdala hyperactivity, prefrontal cortex dysfunction, and altered neurotransmitter systems (GABA, serotonin, norepinephrine).
overview.functional_impact: Persistent worry, restlessness, fatigue, difficulty concentrating, muscle tension, sleep disturbance, avoidance behaviors, reduced quality of life, impaired occupational and social functioning.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~3% of Australian adults (lifelong prevalence ~15%). More common in females. Often comorbid with depression.

screening_tools: Panic Disorder Severity Scale if applicable
assessment.evidence_base: Comprehensive assessment per ESSA Mental Health Position Statement and clinical practice guidelines

exercises.name: Moderate-Intensity Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Reduce anxiety symptoms, improve stress resilience, enhance mood regulation
exercises.modifications: Walking, jogging, cycling, swimming - patient preference important. Outdoor exercise may provide additional benefits.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence for anxiety reduction

exercises.name: Vigorous Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Greater anxiolytic effects than moderate intensity for some individuals
exercises.modifications: May initially increase anxiety in some - monitor response. Build tolerance gradually.

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 8-12 reps, major muscle groups, 2-3x/week
exercises.purpose: Reduce anxiety, improve self-efficacy, physical health
exercises.modifications: Can be standalone or combined with aerobic exercise
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence for anxiety reduction

exercises.name: Yoga
exercises.purpose: Reduce anxiety, mindfulness, relaxation, stress management
exercises.modifications: Various styles effective. Include breathing practices (pranayama).

exercises.name: Tai Chi/Qigong
exercises.purpose: Anxiety reduction, mindfulness, gentle movement
exercises.modifications: Particularly suitable for older adults or those with physical limitations

exercise_prescription.frequency: 3-5 sessions per week - regular routine important for anxiety management
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 6-12 weeks for clinically significant anxiety reduction. Ongoing exercise for sustained benefits.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise has moderate to large anxiolytic effects (SMD -0.6 to -0.9 depending on population and intervention). Effects evident after single sessions (acute anxiety reduction) and with chronic training (trait anxiety reduction). Both aerobic and resistance training effective. Mind-body exercises particularly beneficial for anxiety. Mechanisms include altered neurotransmitter activity, HPA axis regulation, improved sleep, enhanced self-efficacy.

phases.goals: Establish exercise habit, manage initial anxiety about exercise, build confidence
phases.criteria: Start at comfortable intensity. Focus on positive experience. Address exercise-induced anxiety if present.

phases.goals: Build to recommended volumes, optimize anxiety reduction
phases.criteria: Gradually increase to guideline levels. Experiment with modalities. Symptom reduction expected by 6-8 weeks.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise behavior, maintain anxiety management, prevent relapse
phases.criteria: Ongoing regular exercise. Self-management capability. Recognize exercise as anxiety management tool.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach with emphasis on addressing exercise-related anxiety barriers

absolute: Acute panic disorder requiring immediate psychiatric care
Severe comorbid medical conditions limiting exercise
relative: Exercise-induced panic (may need graded exposure approach)
Severe fatigue or energy conservation from anxiety
red_flags: Panic attacks during exercise without apparent cause

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Acute anxiety reduction immediately post-exercise. Clinically significant reduction in trait anxiety within 6-12 weeks.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Anxiety severity: SMD -0.68 (95% CI -0.88 to -0.48) for supervised programs. Effects larger in those with diagnosed anxiety vs. symptoms only.

key_findings: Exercise reduces anxiety symptoms with moderate to large effects
Both aerobic and resistance training effective
Effects comparable to standard treatments (CBT, medications)
Mind-body exercises particularly beneficial
Benefits for both trait and state anxiety
Single sessions provide acute anxiety relief
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Anxiety severity: SMD -0.68 (-0.88 to -0.48). Effects larger for GAD vs. other anxiety disorders. Dose-response relationship present.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate quality evidence from numerous RCTs and meta-analyses. Some heterogeneity in interventions and populations.

references.citation: Aylett E, et al. Exercise in the treatment of clinical anxiety in general practice - a systematic review and meta-analysis. BMC Health Serv Res. 2018
references.key_finding: Exercise effective for anxiety in primary care settings

references.citation: Stubbs B, et al. An examination of the anxiolytic effects of exercise for people with anxiety and stress-related disorders: a meta-analysis. Psychiatry Res. 2017
references.key_finding: Exercise reduces anxiety with moderate to large effects

references.citation: ESSA Position Statement: Exercise and Mental Health. 2021
references.key_finding: Exercise Physiologists have key role in anxiety management through exercise

clinical_note: Screen for panic disorder - may require modified approach. Address exercise-induced anxiety if present (some individuals experience increased anxiety with vigorous exercise). Teach breathing techniques. Exercise adjunct to, not replacement for, psychological treatment in moderate-severe anxiety. Consider group exercise for social support. Collaborate with psychologist/psychiatrist. Assess for comorbid depression (common).

overview.pathophysiology: Mechanical compression of rotator cuff tendons (primarily supraspinatus) and subacromial bursa in subacromial space. Caused by reduced acromiohumeral distance during elevation, often due to altered scapular mechanics, rotator cuff dysfunction, or postural factors.
overview.functional_impact: Shoulder pain with overhead activities, night pain, reduced shoulder strength and range of motion, difficulty with ADLs (reaching, dressing), occupational limitations.
overview.prevalence: Common shoulder condition affecting 18-26% of general population. Peak incidence 40-50 years. Common in overhead athletes and workers.

screening_tools: Red flag screening for serious pathology
assessment.evidence_base: Clinical assessment consistent with best practice shoulder examination per Shoulder PT Clinical Practice Guidelines

exercises.name: Scapular Stabilization Exercises
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 12-15 reps, daily initially then 3-5x/week
exercises.purpose: Restore scapulohumeral rhythm, improve scapular position and control
exercises.modifications: Serratus anterior focus, lower/middle trapezius strengthening, progress from stable to unstable
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate to high quality evidence - key component

exercises.name: Rotator Cuff Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Improve rotator cuff strength and endurance, dynamic stability
exercises.modifications: External rotation emphasis, avoid painful arc initially, progress resistance gradually

exercises.name: Stretching - Posterior Capsule
exercises.purpose: Address posterior capsule tightness contributing to altered mechanics
exercises.modifications: Cross-body stretch, sleeper stretch

exercises.name: Posture Correction Exercises
exercises.purpose: Address forward shoulder posture, thoracic kyphosis
exercises.modifications: Thoracic extension mobilizations, pectoral stretching, postural cueing

exercises.name: Progressive Functional Training
exercises.purpose: Return to functional activities, work/sport-specific demands
exercises.modifications: Gradual return to overhead activities, load management

exercise_prescription.frequency: Daily exercises initially for neuromuscular control, 3-5x/week for strengthening
exercise_prescription.program_duration: 6-12 weeks typical for resolution. Some require longer depending on severity and chronicity.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise therapy is first-line treatment for shoulder impingement. Comparable or superior to surgery in most cases. Scapular and rotator cuff exercises both important. Manual therapy may provide short-term benefits but exercise essential for long-term outcomes. Corticosteroid injections provide short-term pain relief but no long-term benefit over exercise.

phases.goals: Reduce pain and inflammation, restore ROM below 90°
phases.criteria: Pain management, activity modification, gentle ROM. Progress when pain controlled and basic ROM achieved.

phases.goals: Restore neuromuscular control, begin strengthening, improve mechanics
phases.criteria: Scapular stabilization focus, low-resistance rotator cuff work. Progress when movement control improved.

phases.goals: Build strength, return to functional activities
phases.criteria: Progressive resistance, functional training. Progress when strength near normal and minimal pain.

phases.goals: Full function, prevent recurrence
phases.criteria: Sport/work-specific training. Maintenance strengthening 2-3x/week.

progression.evidence_base: Phased approach consistent with tissue healing timelines and best practice rehabilitation

red_flags: Night pain with red flags (fever, weight loss)
History of trauma with suspected fracture

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Pain reduction within 2-4 weeks. Significant functional improvement by 6-12 weeks. Some require 3-6 months.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Pain: SMD -0.27 to -0.81 depending on comparison. Function: SMD -0.37 to -0.92. Exercise superior to no treatment/placebo, comparable to surgery long-term.

key_findings: Exercise therapy effective for shoulder impingement
Comparable outcomes to surgery at 1+ years
Supervised exercise superior to home program alone
Scapular exercises beneficial addition to general shoulder exercise
Manual therapy may provide short-term benefit but exercise essential
Corticosteroid injections no better than exercise long-term
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Pain: SMD -0.69 (95% CI -1.18 to -0.20) vs. placebo. Function: SMD -0.92 (-1.51 to -0.32). Exercise vs. surgery: no significant difference at 1 year.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence from numerous RCTs and systematic reviews

references.citation: Hanratty CE, et al. The effectiveness of physiotherapy exercises in subacromial impingement syndrome: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Semin Arthritis Rheum. 2012
references.key_finding: Exercise therapy effective for improving pain and function in shoulder impingement

references.citation: Ketola S, et al. No evidence of long-term benefits of arthroscopic acromioplasty in the treatment of shoulder impingement syndrome. Bone Joint Res. 2013
references.key_finding: Surgery no better than exercise therapy for shoulder impingement

references.citation: Shoulder Pain Clinical Practice Guidelines. Shoulder PT Coalition. 2020
references.key_finding: Evidence-based recommendations prioritizing exercise therapy

clinical_note: Rule out rotator cuff tear (especially if >60 years or trauma history) - may need imaging. Address workplace ergonomics and activity modification. Emphasize importance of adherence - exercise dosage critical. Many patients improve without surgery. Consider referral to PT if not progressing. Distinguish from cervical radiculopathy, adhesive capsulitis, calcific tendinitis - different management.

overview.pathophysiology: Persistent neck pain without identifiable specific pathology. Multifactorial involving mechanical stress, muscle dysfunction, central sensitization, and psychosocial factors. Often related to postural stress, sedentary work, and psychological factors.
overview.functional_impact: Reduced cervical ROM, headaches, upper limb symptoms, sleep disturbance, impaired work capacity (especially desk workers), reduced quality of life, disability.
overview.prevalence: Affects 10-20% of adults at any time. Lifetime prevalence 30-50%. More common in females and desk workers. Fourth leading cause of disability.

screening_tools: Red flag screening for serious pathology
Yellow flag screening for psychosocial factors
assessment.evidence_base: Assessment consistent with Neck Pain Clinical Practice Guidelines and best evidence

exercises.name: Cervical and Scapular Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Improve neck strength and endurance, reduce pain
exercises.modifications: Include deep neck flexors, extensors, scapular stabilizers. Progress resistance gradually.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - most effective intervention

exercises.name: Deep Neck Flexor Training
exercises.dosage: 2 sets of 10 reps with 10 sec holds, daily
exercises.purpose: Improve deep neck flexor function, postural control
exercises.modifications: Craniocervical flexion, progress pressure targets (20-30 mmHg)

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Overall fitness, pain modulation, psychological benefits
exercises.modifications: Walking, cycling - low impact preferred initially
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence for neck pain

exercises.name: Stretching and ROM Exercises
exercises.dosage: Daily, 30 sec holds, 2-3 reps per direction
exercises.purpose: Maintain/improve cervical mobility
exercises.modifications: All planes of motion, avoid forcing into pain
exercises.evidence_level: Low quality evidence alone, beneficial as part of multimodal

exercises.name: Postural Correction
exercises.purpose: Address forward head posture, reduce mechanical stress
exercises.modifications: Workplace ergonomics, thoracic mobility, postural awareness
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence as part of multimodal program

exercise_prescription.frequency: 3-5 sessions per week minimum, daily for motor control exercises
exercise_prescription.program_duration: 6-12 weeks minimum. May require ongoing management for chronic cases.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise is most effective intervention for chronic neck pain. Strengthening exercises most beneficial. Combined programs (strength, endurance, stretching, aerobic) superior to single interventions. Supervised exercise superior to home exercise alone. Manual therapy provides short-term benefits but exercise essential for long-term outcomes.

phases.goals: Reduce pain, restore basic ROM, address acute factors
phases.criteria: Activity modification, gentle ROM, postural awareness. Progress when pain controlled.

phases.goals: Restore neuromuscular control, begin strengthening
phases.criteria: Deep neck flexor training, low-resistance strengthening. Progress when movement control improved.

phases.goals: Build strength and endurance, functional restoration
phases.criteria: Progressive resistance training. Progress when strength improved, minimal pain with exercises.

phases.goals: Maintain gains, prevent recurrence
phases.criteria: Continued strengthening 2-3x/week, ergonomic adherence, self-management.

progression.evidence_base: Phased progressive approach consistent with best practice rehabilitation and tissue adaptation timelines

relative: Severe acute pain limiting all movement
red_flags: Drop attacks or dizziness with head movement

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Pain reduction typically within 3-6 weeks. Significant functional improvement by 8-12 weeks.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Pain: SMD -0.35 to -0.71 depending on intervention. Disability: SMD -0.36 to -0.69. Strengthening exercises show largest effects.

key_findings: Exercise reduces pain and disability in chronic neck pain
Strengthening exercises most effective single intervention
Combined interventions superior to single modality
Supervised exercise superior to unsupervised
Effects maintained at long-term follow-up with adherence
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Pain immediately post-treatment: SMD -0.71 (95% CI -1.33 to -0.10). Intermediate term: SMD -0.35 (-0.62 to -0.08). Disability: SMD -0.69 (-1.31 to -0.07).
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate quality evidence from multiple RCTs and systematic reviews. Some heterogeneity in interventions.

references.citation: Gross A, et al. Exercise for mechanical neck disorders. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015
references.key_finding: Exercise beneficial for neck pain, particularly strengthening programs

references.citation: Blanpied PR, et al. Neck pain: revision 2017 - Clinical practice guidelines. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 2017
references.key_finding: Comprehensive evidence-based recommendations emphasizing exercise

references.citation: Falla D, et al. Effect of neck exercise on sitting posture in patients with chronic neck pain. Phys Ther. 2007
references.key_finding: Specific neck exercises improve posture and reduce pain

clinical_note: Screen for cervical radiculopathy and myelopathy - different management required. Address workplace ergonomics - critical for office workers. Psychosocial factors important in chronicity. Avoid passive treatments as sole intervention. Emphasize active self-management. Consider sleep posture and pillow selection. Multidisciplinary approach if complex presentation.

overview.pathophysiology: Degenerative condition of Achilles tendon involving failed healing response, disorganized collagen, neovascularization. Not primarily inflammatory despite name 'tendinitis'.
overview.functional_impact: Pain with walking, running, stairs, reduced sports participation, morning stiffness, altered gait, functional limitations.
overview.prevalence: Common in runners and jumping athletes. Also sedentary middle-aged ('weekend warriors'). 6-8% lifetime incidence.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per best practice tendinopathy management guidelines

exercises.name: Heavy Slow Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Tendon remodeling, collagen synthesis - GOLD STANDARD
exercises.modifications: Heel raises off step, bilateral progressing to single-leg, adding weight
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - most effective intervention

exercises.name: Eccentric Loading
exercises.purpose: Tendon adaptation, pain reduction
exercises.modifications: Alfredson protocol - lower heel slowly off step edge
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - traditional gold standard

exercises.name: Isometric Exercise (for pain relief)
exercises.dosage: 5 reps of 45 second holds at moderate load, before activity
exercises.purpose: Acute pain reduction for training/competition
exercises.modifications: Calf raise hold at onset of pain

exercises.name: Calf Stretching
exercises.purpose: Maintain ROM (but NOT primary treatment)
exercises.modifications: Gastrocnemius and soleus
exercises.evidence_level: Low quality evidence - avoid as sole treatment

exercise_prescription.frequency: 3-7 sessions per week depending on protocol
exercise_prescription.program_duration: 12-24 weeks typically for full recovery
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Progressive loading exercises cornerstone of treatment. Heavy slow resistance training and eccentric loading both effective - heavy slow may be superior and better tolerated. Shockwave therapy moderate evidence. Corticosteroid injections should be avoided (risk of rupture). Most respond to conservative management. Surgery rarely needed.

phases.goals: Reduce pain, maintain activity within limits
phases.criteria: Isometric holds, activity modification. Some pain (<5/10) acceptable.

phases.goals: Tendon remodeling through progressive load
phases.criteria: Heavy slow resistance or eccentric program. Progress resistance weekly. Pain during exercise OK if not worsening.

phases.goals: Gradual return to running/sport
phases.criteria: Walk-run program. Plyometric progression. Continued strengthening.

phases.goals: Prevent recurrence
phases.criteria: Continued calf strengthening 2x/week. Load management.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive loading approach based on tendon adaptation timelines and research evidence

relative: Severe acute pain (may need brief rest)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvement typically 6-12 weeks. Full recovery may take 3-6 months.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Heavy slow resistance and eccentric loading: Large effect sizes (d>1.0). Success rates 60-90%.

key_findings: Progressive loading exercises effective for Achilles tendinopathy
Heavy slow resistance may be superior to eccentric
High success rates with conservative management
Shockwave therapy: moderate evidence
Corticosteroid injections: avoid - risk of rupture
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Eccentric training reduces pain and improves function. Heavy slow resistance shows similar or superior outcomes with better adherence.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence for exercise interventions from multiple RCTs

references.citation: Beyer R, et al. Heavy slow resistance versus eccentric training as treatment for Achilles tendinopathy: a randomized controlled trial. Am J Sports Med. 2015
references.key_finding: Heavy slow resistance comparable to eccentric with better patient satisfaction

references.citation: Malliaras P, et al. Achilles and patellar tendinopathy loading programmes: a systematic review comparing clinical outcomes. Br J Sports Med. 2013
references.key_finding: Loading programs effective for tendinopathy

clinical_note: Avoid corticosteroid injections (rupture risk). Educate on realistic timelines - patience required. Address biomechanical factors, footwear, training loads. Some pain during exercise is acceptable and expected - tendon needs loading. 'No pain no gain' within reason. Refer for imaging if not improving by 12 weeks or if suspecting tear.

overview.pathophysiology: Syndrome of impaired cardiac pump function resulting in inadequate tissue perfusion. May be systolic (reduced ejection fraction, HFrEF) or diastolic (preserved EF, HFpEF) dysfunction. Progressive condition with neurohumoral activation.
overview.functional_impact: Severe exercise intolerance, dyspnea, fatigue, fluid retention, reduced quality of life, frequent hospitalizations, high mortality risk.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~2% of Australian adults, increasing to ~10% in those >75 years. Leading cause of hospitalization in older adults.

key_assessments: Cardiopulmonary exercise test (CPET) with peak VO2
assessment.evidence_base: Comprehensive assessment per Heart Foundation guidelines and international HF exercise standards

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise - Continuous
exercises.dosage: 20-30 minutes at 40-70% peak VO2 or HRR, 3-5x/week
exercises.purpose: Improve exercise capacity, reduce symptoms, enhance quality of life
exercises.modifications: Cycling often better tolerated than walking initially. Start low, progress slowly.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - improves outcomes and reduces hospitalizations

exercises.name: Interval Training
exercises.dosage: 30-60 sec work intervals at 80-90% peak, equal rest, 20-30 min total, 2-3x/week
exercises.purpose: Superior VO2peak improvements, well tolerated
exercises.modifications: Only for stable patients. Close monitoring initially.
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate to high quality evidence - safe in stable HF

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 1-2 sets of 10-15 reps at 40-60% 1RM, major muscle groups, 2-3x/week
exercises.purpose: Combat muscle wasting, improve functional capacity, complement aerobic
exercises.modifications: Lower intensity than for healthy adults. Avoid valsalva. Circuit style effective.
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence - safe and beneficial

exercises.name: Inspiratory Muscle Training
exercises.dosage: 30 breaths at 30-50% max inspiratory pressure, 5-7 days/week
exercises.purpose: Improve inspiratory muscle strength, reduce dyspnea
exercises.modifications: Use threshold IMT device. Progress resistance weekly.

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 12 weeks supervised, ongoing maintenance essential for sustained benefits
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise training reduces hospitalizations by 30%, improves quality of life and functional capacity. Peak VO2 increases of 15-20% typical. Benefits require ongoing exercise - effects diminish with cessation. Combined aerobic and resistance training recommended.

phases.goals: Assess safety, optimize medications, establish baseline
phases.criteria: Clinically stable (no recent decompensation). NYHA II-III ideal. Medical clearance essential.

phases.goals: Improve exercise capacity, symptom management, education
phases.criteria: Close monitoring (BP, symptoms, rhythm if indicated). Slow progression given cardiac limitations.

phases.goals: Develop independent exercise capability, community resources
phases.criteria: Reduce supervision, establish home/community program, action plan for symptoms.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise levels, prevent decompensation, monitor
phases.criteria: Minimum 3-4x/week exercise. Periodic reassessments. Rapid access for worsening symptoms.

progression.evidence_base: Supervised exercise training recommended by international HF guidelines (ESC, AHA)

relative: NYHA IV (very limited exercise, requires special protocols)
Atrial fibrillation with poor rate control

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvements in exercise capacity within 4-8 weeks. QoL improvements earlier. Hospitalization reduction over 6-12 months.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Peak VO2: +2.16 ml/kg/min (95% CI 1.7-2.7). HF hospitalizations: RR 0.70 (0.60-0.83). QoL: significant improvements across measures.

key_findings: Exercise training safe and effective in stable CHF
Reduces HF hospitalizations by ~30%
Improves exercise capacity and quality of life
Effects on mortality promising but require confirmation
Benefits maintained with continued exercise
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: All-cause mortality: trend toward reduction but not significant. HF hospitalization: RR 0.70 (0.60-0.83). Peak VO2: +2.16 ml/kg/min. QoL: large improvements.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from multiple large RCTs including HF-ACTION trial

references.citation: O'Connor CM, et al. Efficacy and safety of exercise training in patients with chronic heart failure: HF-ACTION randomized controlled trial. JAMA. 2009;301:1439-50
references.key_finding: Exercise training safe, reduces HF hospitalizations, improves QoL

references.citation: Taylor RS, et al. Exercise-based rehabilitation for heart failure. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2019
references.key_finding: Exercise reduces HF hospitalizations and improves quality of life

references.citation: National Heart Foundation Australia. Exercise in Chronic Heart Failure. 2020
references.key_finding: Australian recommendations for safe exercise prescription in CHF

clinical_note: Daily weight monitoring essential - educate on 2kg gain rule. Medication timing important (beta-blockers limit HR response). Screen for depression (50% prevalence). Ensure understanding of warning signs. Multidisciplinary approach with cardiologist, HF nurse, EP. Close monitoring initially given safety considerations.

overview.pathophysiology: Progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by dopaminergic neuron loss in substantia nigra. Results in motor symptoms (tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, postural instability) and non-motor symptoms (cognitive, autonomic, mood).
overview.functional_impact: Impaired mobility and gait, increased falls risk, reduced independence, freezing of gait, fatigue, depression, reduced quality of life. Progressive functional decline.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~1 in 500 Australians, ~1 in 100 over age 60. Second most common neurodegenerative disease after Alzheimer's.

assessment.evidence_base: Comprehensive assessment recommended by Movement Disorders Society and Parkinson's Australia guidelines

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.dosage: 30-40 minutes at moderate to vigorous intensity, 3-5x/week
exercises.purpose: Neuroprotection, improve cardiovascular fitness, motor symptoms, mood
exercises.modifications: Treadmill, cycling, dancing - vary to maintain engagement. Music/rhythm cues beneficial.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - cornerstone of PD management

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 8-12 reps at 60-80% 1RM, 2-3x/week, major muscle groups
exercises.purpose: Improve strength, power, functional mobility, counter muscle rigidity
exercises.modifications: Include power training (fast concentric phase) - particularly beneficial for PD

exercises.name: Balance and Gait Training
exercises.purpose: Reduce falls risk, improve postural stability, gait quality
exercises.modifications: Include dual-task training, multidirectional stepping, perturbations, obstacle courses
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence for falls reduction

exercises.name: Flexibility and Range of Motion
exercises.dosage: Daily, 30-60 sec holds, focus on trunk and hips
exercises.purpose: Counter rigidity, maintain mobility, postural alignment
exercises.modifications: Trunk rotation, hip flexor stretching particularly important

exercises.name: Boxing/Dance
exercises.purpose: Motor learning, coordination, cognitive engagement, social
exercises.modifications: Rock Steady Boxing program, tango dancing - evidence-based PD programs
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate to high quality evidence - growing support

exercises.name: Cueing Strategies
exercises.purpose: Overcome freezing, improve gait initiation and rhythm
exercises.modifications: Visual (floor markers), auditory (metronome), verbal cues

exercise_prescription.frequency: 4-7 days per week - daily activity recommended
exercise_prescription.session_duration: 45-90 minutes total (can be broken into shorter bouts)
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Ongoing lifelong intervention. Benefits maintained only with continued exercise.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise is disease-modifying intervention showing neuroprotective effects. High-intensity exercise particularly beneficial for motor symptoms. Combined aerobic, strength, and balance training superior to single modality. Dose-response relationship exists - more is better. Task-specific training critical for functional outcomes.

phases.goals: Comprehensive baseline, optimize medication timing for exercise, education
phases.criteria: Exercise during 'ON' periods when medication effective. Assess falls risk, cognitive status.

phases.goals: Establish exercise routine, motor learning, build confidence
phases.criteria: Moderate intensity initially. Focus on technique and safety. Progress when consistently attending.

phases.goals: Increase intensity and complexity, maximize benefits
phases.criteria: Progress toward high-intensity exercise. Add complexity and challenge. Symptom improvement expected.

phases.goals: Sustain high exercise levels, prevent/delay progression, maintain function
phases.criteria: Minimum 150 min/week moderate-vigorous activity. Varied activities. Ongoing progression as able.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive high-intensity approach supported by emerging research on neuroprotection

absolute: Severe uncontrolled dyskinesias interfering with safe movement
red_flags: Atypical features suggesting other parkinsonian syndromes

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Motor symptom improvements within 6-8 weeks. Functional benefits within 8-12 weeks. Neuroprotective effects long-term.
key_outcomes: Improved UPDRS motor scores (15-20% improvement possible)
outcomes.effect_sizes: UPDRS motor: SMD -0.41 (95% CI -0.58 to -0.23). Balance: SMD 0.37. QoL: SMD 0.26. Falls: reduced by 25-40% in some studies.

key_findings: Exercise improves motor symptoms, balance, gait, and QoL
High-intensity exercise shows superior benefits
Task-specific training critical for functional outcomes
Dance and boxing programs particularly effective
Exercise may have neuroprotective effects
Benefits require ongoing participation
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: UPDRS motor: SMD -0.41 (-0.58 to -0.23). Balance (Berg): MD 5.0 points. Gait speed: MD 0.08 m/s. QoL: SMD 0.26.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence from multiple RCTs and systematic reviews

references.citation: Tomlinson CL, et al. Physiotherapy for Parkinson's disease: a comparison of techniques. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014
references.key_finding: Physiotherapy interventions improve motor function and balance in PD

references.citation: Schenkman M, et al. Effect of high-intensity treadmill exercise on motor symptoms in patients with de novo Parkinson disease: a phase 2 randomized clinical trial. JAMA Neurol. 2018
references.key_finding: High-intensity exercise shows potential disease-modifying effects in early PD

references.citation: Parkinson's Australia. Living well with Parkinson's - Exercise Guidelines. 2021
references.key_finding: Australian recommendations emphasizing exercise as core intervention

clinical_note: Time exercise sessions with medication (during 'ON' periods). Falls risk high - ensure safe environment. Screen for orthostatic hypotension. Monitor for dyskinesias during exercise. Cognitive impairment may affect motor learning - adapt accordingly. Depression common - screen regularly. Multidisciplinary approach essential (neurologist, PT, EP, OT, SLP).

overview.pathophysiology: Multifactorial syndrome involving age-related decline in balance, strength, proprioception, vision, cognition, and medication effects. Not a disease but consequence of multiple risk factors.
overview.functional_impact: Injuries from falls, fear of falling, activity restriction, loss of independence, institutionalization, increased mortality. Falls are leading cause of injury-related death in older adults.
overview.prevalence: ~30% of adults >65 years fall annually, increasing to 50% in those >80. Falls account for 40% of injury-related deaths.

key_assessments: Falls history and risk factor screen
assessment.evidence_base: Comprehensive multifactorial assessment recommended by WHO and Australian Falls Prevention Guidelines

exercises.name: Progressive Balance Training
exercises.purpose: Improve balance, reduce falls risk - most critical component
exercises.modifications: Reduce base of support, add dynamic movements, perturbations, dual-task, eyes closed. Progress systematically.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - reduces falls by ~24%

exercises.name: Lower Limb Strengthening
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 8-12 reps, 2-3x/week, moderate to high intensity
exercises.purpose: Improve leg strength, power, functional capacity
exercises.modifications: Focus on major lower limb muscles - squats, leg press, calf raises. Include power training (fast movements).
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence when combined with balance training

exercises.name: Tai Chi
exercises.purpose: Improve balance, strength, proprioception, confidence
exercises.modifications: Group classes beneficial. Various styles effective (Yang style most studied).
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - reduces falls by ~43%

exercises.name: Gait Training
exercises.purpose: Improve gait quality, speed, adaptability
exercises.modifications: Include varied terrain, dual-task walking, obstacle navigation

exercises.name: Flexibility
exercises.purpose: Maintain mobility, ankle range of motion
exercises.modifications: Focus on hip, knee, ankle mobility
exercises.evidence_level: Low quality as standalone, beneficial as part of multimodal program

exercise_prescription.frequency: Balance training minimum 3x/week (daily ideal), strength 2-3x/week, total 3+ hours/week
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 12 weeks to show falls reduction benefit. Ongoing exercise essential - effects cease when stopping.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise reduces falls by 23-24% and injurious falls by 27-30%. Programs must include balance challenge and be >3 months duration with ongoing participation. Balance + strength training most effective. Higher dose (>3 hours/week) superior. Tai Chi particularly effective stand-alone intervention. Group and individual programs both effective.

phases.goals: Comprehensive falls risk assessment, establish safe baseline
phases.criteria: Identify fall risk factors. Address medical issues, medications, vision, footwear, home hazards.

phases.goals: Build balance, strength, confidence. Establish exercise habit.
phases.criteria: Supervised exercise 2-3x/week. Progressive challenge. Home exercise program. Monitor for adverse events.

phases.goals: Maintain gains, transition to independence
phases.criteria: Reduce supervision. Community exercise options. Ongoing home program. Continued progression.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise, prevent falls, maintain independence
phases.criteria: Minimum 3-5 hours/week exercise. Annual reassessments. Rapid intervention if fall occurs.

progression.evidence_base: Minimum 12-week programs required for falls reduction per Cochrane review. Ongoing exercise essential.

absolute: Severe cognitive impairment limiting safety awareness

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Balance improvements within 4-8 weeks. Falls reduction evident after 3+ months of intervention.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Falls rate: RR 0.77 (95% CI 0.71-0.83). Risk of falling: RR 0.85 (0.81-0.89). Injurious falls: RR 0.73 (0.63-0.85). Exercise alone when balance challenged.

key_findings: Exercise reduces falls by 23-24%
Balance and functional exercises essential component
Strength training enhances but insufficient alone
Tai Chi reduces falls by 43%
Higher dose (>3 hrs/week) more effective
Group and home programs both effective
Multifactorial interventions reduce falls by 29%
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Falls rate: RR 0.77 (0.71-0.83). Risk of falling: RR 0.85 (0.81-0.89). Exercise programs including balance: RR 0.76. Tai Chi: RR 0.57.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and Cochrane reviews involving >100,000 participants

references.citation: Sherrington C, et al. Exercise for preventing falls in older people living in the community. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2019
references.key_finding: Exercise reduces falls by 23%. Balance challenge and higher dose most effective.

references.citation: Gillespie LD, et al. Interventions for preventing falls in older people living in the community. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2012
references.key_finding: Multifactorial interventions reduce falls by 29%

references.citation: Australian Commission on Safety and Quality in Health Care. Preventing Falls and Harm From Falls in Older People. 2023
references.key_finding: Australian guidelines for comprehensive falls prevention

clinical_note: Multifactorial approach essential - address ALL risk factors (medications, vision, home hazards, footwear, vitamin D, osteoporosis). Medication review critical (especially psychotropics, antihypertensives). Vision check. Home assessment by OT if multiple falls. Fracture risk assessment. Post-fall assessment essential. Educate on getting up after a fall. Multidisciplinary team approach.

overview.pathophysiology: Non-specific low back pain (NSLBP) refers to pain in the lumbar region without identifiable pathoanatomical cause. Involves mechanical stress, inflammatory processes, and neural sensitization. May include muscle strain, ligamentous injury, or facet joint irritation.
overview.functional_impact: Reduced spinal mobility, impaired lifting capacity, difficulty with prolonged sitting/standing, sleep disturbance, reduced work capacity, and decreased quality of life.
overview.prevalence: Lifetime prevalence of 60-80% in Australian adults. Leading cause of disability globally and major contributor to work absenteeism.

screening_tools: Red flag screening for serious pathology
Yellow flag screening for psychosocial risk factors
