assessment.evidence_base: Strong evidence for comprehensive assessment including physical and psychosocial factors (NICE guidelines 2020, Australian Acute Musculoskeletal Pain Guidelines)

exercises.name: Motor Control Exercises
exercises.purpose: Restore coordinated movement patterns and spinal stability
exercises.modifications: Progress from supine to standing, add instability surfaces

exercises.name: Graded Activity/Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Reduce disability, improve overall fitness, reduce fear-avoidance
exercises.modifications: Walking, cycling, swimming - patient preference important

exercises.name: Trunk Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Improve trunk muscle endurance and strength
exercises.modifications: Progress from isometric to dynamic, add resistance gradually

exercises.name: Flexibility Exercises
exercises.purpose: Maintain/improve range of motion
exercises.modifications: Focus on hip flexors, hamstrings, thoracic spine
exercises.evidence_level: Low to moderate quality evidence as standalone, beneficial as adjunct

exercise_prescription.program_duration: 6-12 weeks minimum, ongoing maintenance recommended
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise therapy is effective for chronic NSLBP with small to moderate effect sizes. No single exercise type superior - individual tailoring important (Cochrane Review 2019).

phases.goals: Pain management, maintain activity levels, prevent chronicity
phases.criteria: Advice to stay active, gentle movement, avoid bed rest. Progress when pain allows basic ADLs.

phases.goals: Restore normal movement patterns, gradually increase activity
phases.criteria: Introduce structured exercise, motor control work. Progress when can perform exercises without pain flare-ups.

phases.goals: Optimize function, build capacity, self-management
phases.criteria: Progressive strengthening, return to full activity. Progress based on functional goals achievement.

progression.evidence_base: Graded activity approach supported by clinical guidelines (NICE 2020, Australian AMBP Guidelines 2019)

relative: Severe acute pain limiting all movement

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvement in pain and function typically seen within 4-8 weeks with consistent adherence
success_indicators: Ability to perform desired activities without significant pain
outcomes.effect_sizes: Moderate effect sizes for pain (SMD -0.35 to -0.70) and function (SMD -0.45 to -0.75) compared to minimal intervention

key_findings: Exercise more effective than usual care for chronic LBP
Motor control exercises effective for recurrent LBP
Combining exercise with education improves outcomes
Any exercise better than no exercise
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Pain reduction: SMD -0.49 (95% CI -0.69 to -0.29). Disability reduction: SMD -0.52 (95% CI -0.69 to -0.35)
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence from multiple systematic reviews and RCTs

references.citation: Hayden JA, et al. Exercise therapy for chronic low back pain. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2021;9:CD009790
references.key_finding: Exercise therapy effective for reducing pain and improving function in chronic LBP

references.citation: NICE Guidelines. Low back pain and sciatica in over 16s: assessment and management. NG59. 2020
references.key_finding: Exercise programs should be tailored to individual and include aerobic, strengthening, and flexibility components

references.citation: Saragiotto BT, et al. Motor control exercise for chronic non-specific low back pain. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2016
references.key_finding: Motor control exercise reduces pain and disability more than minimal intervention

clinical_note: Emphasize biopsychosocial approach. Address fear-avoidance beliefs and catastrophizing. Patient education about pain mechanisms crucial. Avoid over-medicalization.

overview.pathophysiology: Degenerative joint disease involving cartilage breakdown, subchondral bone changes, synovial inflammation, and osteophyte formation. Progressive loss of joint structure and function.
overview.functional_impact: Pain with weight-bearing activities, reduced walking capacity, stair climbing difficulty, impaired balance, reduced physical activity levels, risk of falls.
overview.prevalence: Affects 10-15% of Australian adults >45 years. Leading cause of disability in older adults. More common in females and with obesity.

outcome_measures: WOMAC (Western Ontario McMaster Universities OA Index)
Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS)
assessment.evidence_base: Core set of outcome measures recommended by OARSI (Osteoarthritis Research Society International)

exercises.name: Quadriceps Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Improve knee stability, reduce pain, enhance function
exercises.modifications: Progress from isometric to isotonic, add resistance bands/weights

exercises.name: Lower Limb Strengthening (Hip, Ankle)
exercises.purpose: Address kinetic chain dysfunction, improve overall leg strength
exercises.modifications: Include hip abduction, adduction, calf raises

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Improve cardiovascular fitness, weight management, reduce systemic inflammation
exercises.modifications: Low-impact options: cycling, swimming, water aerobics

exercises.name: Balance and Proprioception
exercises.purpose: Reduce fall risk, improve joint position sense
exercises.modifications: Progress from two-leg to single-leg, eyes closed, unstable surfaces

exercises.name: Range of Motion Exercises
exercises.purpose: Maintain joint mobility, reduce stiffness
exercises.modifications: Active and active-assisted ROM, avoid forcing into pain

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 8-12 weeks to see benefits, ongoing long-term recommended
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise is a core treatment for knee OA with similar effect sizes to NSAIDs for pain relief. Land-based exercise superior to water-based for long-term outcomes. Combination of strengthening and aerobic exercise most effective.

phases.goals: Reduce pain, improve basic strength, establish exercise habit
phases.criteria: Low resistance, focus on technique, pain monitoring. Progress when exercises performed correctly without flare-ups.

phases.goals: Build strength and endurance, increase function
phases.criteria: Increase resistance/duration, add complexity. Progress when strength improves and functional capacity increases.

phases.goals: Maximize strength, optimize function, return to desired activities
phases.criteria: Higher intensity, functional exercises, sports-specific if relevant. Transition to maintenance program.

phases.goals: Maintain gains, prevent deterioration
phases.criteria: Minimum 2-3x/week structured exercise, ongoing activity modification as needed

progression.evidence_base: Progressive resistance training principles supported by OARSI guidelines and multiple RCTs

red_flags: Suspected infection (hot, red, swollen joint with fever)
Locking or giving way suggesting mechanical obstruction

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Pain and function improvements typically seen within 6-8 weeks, with continued improvement up to 6 months
outcomes.effect_sizes: Moderate effect sizes: pain SMD -0.49, function SMD -0.52 compared to control. Effects comparable to NSAIDs.

key_findings: Exercise reduces pain and improves function in knee OA
Strengthening exercises most effective single intervention
Combination programs superior to single-modality
Benefits maintained with ongoing exercise
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Pain: SMD -0.49 (95% CI -0.39 to -0.59). Function: SMD -0.52 (95% CI -0.39 to -0.64). Effect sizes similar to NSAIDs.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from multiple systematic reviews, meta-analyses, and large RCTs

references.citation: Fransen M, et al. Exercise for osteoarthritis of the knee. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015;1:CD004376
references.key_finding: Land-based exercise reduces pain and improves function in knee OA

references.citation: Bannuru RR, et al. OARSI guidelines for non-surgical management of knee, hip, and polyarticular OA. Osteoarthritis Cartilage. 2019;27:1578-89
references.key_finding: Exercise strongly recommended as core treatment for all patients with knee OA

references.citation: Skou ST, et al. Good Life with osteoArthritis in Denmark (GLA:D): evidence-based education and supervised neuromuscular exercise. BMC Musculoskelet Disord. 2017;18:72
references.key_finding: Structured education and exercise program shows sustained benefits at 12 months

clinical_note: Education critical - address misconceptions about 'wear and tear'. Exercise is safe and beneficial. Weight loss important if overweight/obese (5-10% reduction clinically meaningful). Consider referral to APD.

overview.pathophysiology: Chronic metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency, leading to hyperglycemia. Progressive beta-cell dysfunction. Associated with obesity, physical inactivity, and genetic factors.
overview.functional_impact: Fatigue, reduced exercise tolerance, increased cardiovascular risk, peripheral neuropathy, retinopathy, nephropathy complications. Impacts quality of life and life expectancy.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~5-6% of Australian adults (1.3 million). Rapidly increasing prevalence. Higher rates in older adults, Indigenous Australians, and those with obesity.

assessment.evidence_base: Comprehensive assessment recommended by Diabetes Australia and ESSA Position Statements

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.dosage: 150 minutes/week moderate intensity OR 75 minutes vigorous intensity
exercises.purpose: Improve glycemic control, cardiovascular fitness, weight management
exercises.modifications: Walking, cycling, swimming - spread across ≥3 days/week, no more than 2 consecutive days without exercise

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sessions/week, 2-3 sets of 8-12 reps, major muscle groups
exercises.purpose: Improve insulin sensitivity, increase muscle mass, glycemic control
exercises.modifications: Progress from body weight to resistance bands to free weights/machines

exercises.name: High-Intensity Interval Training (HIIT)
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sessions/week, alternating high (80-90% HRmax) and low intensity intervals
exercises.purpose: Efficient glycemic control improvement, cardiovascular fitness
exercises.modifications: Start with shorter intervals (30-60 sec), progress duration and intensity
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate to high quality evidence - superior to continuous moderate exercise for HbA1c reduction

exercises.name: Flexibility and Balance
exercises.purpose: Maintain mobility, prevent falls (especially if neuropathy present)
exercises.modifications: Include yoga, tai chi as alternatives
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence for balance/falls prevention

exercise_prescription.frequency: At least 3 days/week for aerobic (ideally 5), 2-3 days/week for resistance
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Ongoing lifelong intervention. Significant improvements within 8-12 weeks.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Combined aerobic and resistance training superior to either alone for glycemic control. Exercise reduces HbA1c by 0.6-0.8% on average. Benefits require ongoing participation. Reduces cardiovascular mortality by 30-40%.

phases.goals: Establish exercise habit, improve basic fitness, monitor glucose response
phases.criteria: Start at low-moderate intensity, frequent glucose monitoring. Progress when stable glucose responses achieved.

phases.goals: Increase exercise volume and intensity, optimize glycemic control
phases.criteria: Gradually increase to recommended volumes. Progress when meeting aerobic and strength guidelines without adverse effects.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise levels, maintain glycemic control, prevent complications
phases.criteria: Continue meeting physical activity guidelines, regular HbA1c monitoring, adjust as needed

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach supported by ADA/ESSA guidelines with emphasis on minimizing sedentary time

absolute: Severe proliferative retinopathy (risk of vitreous hemorrhage)
Severe autonomic neuropathy with cardiovascular involvement

outcomes.expected_timeframe: HbA1c improvements typically seen within 8-12 weeks. Cardiovascular fitness improvements within 4-8 weeks.
success_indicators: HbA1c reduction toward target (<7% for most)
outcomes.effect_sizes: Aerobic exercise: HbA1c reduction ~0.6% (95% CI -0.8 to -0.4). Resistance training: ~0.57%. Combined: ~0.8%

key_findings: Exercise reduces HbA1c independent of weight loss
Combined aerobic + resistance training most effective
Structured programs superior to advice alone
Higher volumes associated with greater benefits
Exercise reduces cardiovascular mortality
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: HbA1c reduction: -0.67% (95% CI -0.84 to -0.49) for structured programs. Cardiovascular mortality reduction: HR 0.60-0.70.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from numerous RCTs and systematic reviews

references.citation: Colberg SR, et al. Physical activity/exercise and diabetes: a position statement of the ADA. Diabetes Care. 2016;39:2065-79
references.key_finding: Exercise is fundamental component of diabetes management. Combined aerobic and resistance training recommended.

references.citation: Umpierre D, et al. Physical activity advice only or structured exercise training and association with HbA1c in type 2 diabetes. JAMA. 2011;305:1790-9
references.key_finding: Structured exercise training reduces HbA1c by 0.89% vs 0.36% for advice only

references.citation: ESSA Position Statement: Exercise and Type 2 Diabetes. 2020
references.key_finding: Exercise Physiologists play key role in diabetes management through exercise prescription

clinical_note: Monitor blood glucose before and after exercise initially. Educate on hypoglycemia risk and management. Consider medication adjustments with prescriber. Emphasize reducing sedentary time. Multidisciplinary approach essential - coordinate with GP, endocrinologist, APD.

overview.pathophysiology: Atherosclerotic narrowing of coronary arteries leading to myocardial ischemia. May present as stable angina, unstable angina, or myocardial infarction. Associated with endothelial dysfunction and inflammation.
overview.functional_impact: Reduced exercise capacity, fatigue, chest pain with exertion, anxiety, depression, reduced quality of life. May limit occupational and recreational activities.
overview.prevalence: Leading cause of death in Australia. Affects ~3-5% of adults. Higher prevalence in older age groups and males.

key_assessments: Graded exercise test (GXT) with ECG monitoring
assessment.evidence_base: Comprehensive assessment recommended by AACVPR, ACRA guidelines prior to cardiac rehabilitation

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise - Continuous
exercises.purpose: Improve cardiorespiratory fitness, reduce cardiac risk factors
exercises.modifications: Walking, cycling, rowing - telemetry monitoring initially if high risk
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - cornerstone of cardiac rehabilitation

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise - Interval Training
exercises.dosage: 4x4 minutes at 85-95% peak HR, 3 min active recovery, 2-3x/week
exercises.purpose: Superior improvements in VO2peak, endothelial function
exercises.modifications: Only for stable, low-moderate risk patients after initial conditioning
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - superior to moderate continuous training

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Improve muscular strength, functional capacity, bone health
exercises.modifications: Avoid valsalva maneuver, start with low resistance, monitor BP response
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate to high quality evidence - safe and effective when supervised

exercises.name: Flexibility Exercises
exercises.purpose: Maintain mobility, complement other training
exercises.modifications: Static stretches, avoid straining
exercises.evidence_level: Low quality evidence - recommended as adjunct

exercise_prescription.frequency: 3-5 sessions per week (aerobic), 2-3 sessions per week (resistance)
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 12 weeks supervised cardiac rehabilitation, ongoing maintenance exercise lifelong
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Cardiac rehabilitation exercise programs reduce cardiovascular mortality by 26% and hospital readmissions by 18%. HIIT shows superior improvements in VO2peak (15-20% vs 10-12% for moderate continuous). Comprehensive programs including exercise, education, and psychological support most effective.

phases.goals: Early mobilization, prevent deconditioning
phases.criteria: Progress from sitting to walking short distances, 1-2 METs. Medical clearance required.

phases.goals: Improve fitness, education, risk factor modification, psychological support
phases.criteria: Supervised exercise with ECG monitoring (if indicated). Progress intensity gradually based on symptoms, HR, ECG response.

phases.goals: Maintain fitness gains, sustain lifestyle changes, secondary prevention
phases.criteria: Independent or group-based exercise. Periodic reassessments. Ongoing risk factor management.

progression.evidence_base: Phased approach is standard of care internationally (AACVPR, ACRA guidelines)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Significant improvements in exercise capacity within 8-12 weeks. Mortality benefits accrue over months to years.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Mortality reduction: RR 0.74 (95% CI 0.64-0.86). VO2peak increase: 15-20% with HIIT, 10-15% with moderate continuous training.

key_findings: Exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation reduces cardiovascular mortality by 26%
HIIT superior to moderate continuous training for VO2peak
Comprehensive programs (exercise + education) superior to exercise alone
Benefits maintained long-term with adherence
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Cardiovascular mortality: RR 0.74 (0.64-0.86). All-cause mortality: RR 0.80 (0.68-0.93). Hospital readmissions: RR 0.82 (0.70-0.96).
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and systematic reviews

references.citation: Anderson L, et al. Exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation for coronary heart disease. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2016;1:CD001800
references.key_finding: Exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation reduces cardiovascular mortality and hospital admissions

references.citation: Rognmo Ø, et al. Cardiovascular risk of high- versus moderate-intensity aerobic exercise in coronary heart disease patients. Circulation. 2012;126:1436-40
references.key_finding: HIIT safe and superior to moderate exercise for improving VO2peak

references.citation: ACRA Standards for Exercise Delivery in Cardiovascular Disease. 2020
references.key_finding: Australian guidelines for safe and effective cardiac rehabilitation delivery

clinical_note: Ensure medical clearance before starting. ECG monitoring recommended for high-risk patients initially. Teach symptom recognition and management. Emphasize secondary prevention (smoking cessation, diet, medication adherence). Multidisciplinary approach essential.

overview.pathophysiology: Progressive airflow limitation due to chronic inflammation of airways and lung parenchyma. Includes emphysema and chronic bronchitis. Characterized by airflow obstruction, gas trapping, and ventilatory impairment.
overview.functional_impact: Dyspnea, reduced exercise tolerance, fatigue, frequent exacerbations, social isolation, anxiety/depression, impaired quality of life. Progressive functional decline.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~7-8% of Australian adults >40 years. Fourth leading cause of death globally. Strongly associated with smoking history.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment protocols recommended by GOLD guidelines and Lung Foundation Australia

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise - Continuous
exercises.dosage: 20-40 minutes at 60-80% peak work rate or 4-6 Borg scale, 3-5x/week
exercises.purpose: Improve exercise capacity, reduce dyspnea, enhance quality of life
exercises.modifications: Walking, cycling - interval training if continuous too difficult initially
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - cornerstone of pulmonary rehabilitation

exercises.name: Interval Training
exercises.dosage: 30-60 sec work at 80-100% peak, 30-60 sec rest, 20-30 min total, 3x/week
exercises.purpose: Alternative for severe COPD, less dyspnea than continuous
exercises.modifications: Better tolerated than continuous in severe COPD

exercises.name: Resistance Training - Upper Body
exercises.purpose: Improve ADL capacity, arm function
exercises.modifications: Focus on shoulder girdle, arms - functional movements

exercises.name: Resistance Training - Lower Body
exercises.purpose: Improve leg strength, walking capacity, prevent muscle wasting
exercises.modifications: Leg press, squats, leg extension - critical for function

exercises.name: Breathing Exercises
exercises.dosage: Pursed-lip breathing, diaphragmatic breathing practice daily
exercises.purpose: Symptom management, reduce dyspnea
exercises.modifications: Teach early, practice during exercise
exercises.evidence_level: Low to moderate quality evidence as standalone

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 8 weeks supervised pulmonary rehabilitation. Ongoing maintenance critical.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Pulmonary rehabilitation is most effective non-pharmacological intervention for COPD. Reduces dyspnea, improves exercise capacity and quality of life. Effects diminish after cessation - maintenance essential. Combined aerobic and strength training superior to aerobic alone.

phases.goals: Baseline assessment, optimize medication, teach breathing techniques
phases.criteria: Clinically stable (no recent exacerbation within 4 weeks). Medical clearance obtained.

phases.goals: Improve exercise capacity, dyspnea management, education
phases.criteria: 2-3 supervised sessions per week. Progress intensity based on symptom response and SpO2 monitoring.

phases.goals: Develop independent exercise capability, self-management
phases.criteria: Reduce supervision gradually, establish home program, community exercise options.

phases.goals: Sustain benefits, prevent decline, manage exacerbations
phases.criteria: Minimum 3x/week exercise. Annual reassessments. Rapid access for exacerbations.

progression.evidence_base: 8-week minimum duration for pulmonary rehabilitation supported by international guidelines (ATS/ERS, COPD-X)

red_flags: SpO2 <88% during exercise despite supplemental O2
Severe dyspnea out of proportion to exercise

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Significant improvements in 6MWD and QoL within 6-8 weeks. Dyspnea reduction evident within 4 weeks.
outcomes.effect_sizes: 6MWD: +44m (95% CI 33-55m). QoL: SMD 0.48-0.78 depending on measure. Exacerbations: reduced by ~30%.

key_findings: Pulmonary rehabilitation improves exercise capacity, QoL, and dyspnea
Benefits maintained up to 12 months with ongoing exercise
Resistance training enhances outcomes
Early rehabilitation post-exacerbation beneficial
Home-based programs less effective than supervised
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: 6MWD: +44m (33-55m). SGRQ: -7 units (-9.3 to -4.7). Dyspnea: SMD -0.79 (-1.01 to -0.56). Hospital admissions: RR 0.70 (0.56-0.88).
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and Cochrane reviews

references.citation: McCarthy B, et al. Pulmonary rehabilitation for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015;2:CD003793
references.key_finding: Pulmonary rehabilitation significantly improves exercise capacity, symptoms, and quality of life

references.citation: COPD-X Guidelines. Concise Guide for Primary Care. Lung Foundation Australia. 2023
references.key_finding: Australian evidence-based recommendations for COPD management including exercise

references.citation: Spruit MA, et al. An official ATS/ERS statement: key concepts and advances in pulmonary rehabilitation. AJRCCM. 2013;188:e13-64
references.key_finding: Comprehensive international standards for pulmonary rehabilitation

clinical_note: Oxygen supplementation if SpO2 <88% during exercise. Teach breathing strategies (pursed-lip breathing). Monitor for exacerbations. Smoking cessation critical. Education on medication compliance, energy conservation, action plans essential. Multidisciplinary team approach.

overview.pathophysiology: Cerebrovascular accident resulting in focal brain injury from ischemia or hemorrhage. Leads to motor impairment, sensory deficits, cognitive changes, and functional limitations based on lesion location and extent.
overview.functional_impact: Hemiparesis/hemiplegia, impaired balance and gait, reduced cardiovascular fitness, spasticity, fatigue, cognitive deficits, reduced independence in ADLs.
overview.prevalence: Stroke affects ~50,000 Australians annually. Leading cause of disability. 65% have persisting impairments affecting daily function.

assessment.evidence_base: Core assessment battery recommended by Stroke Foundation Australia Clinical Guidelines

exercises.name: Task-Specific Gait Training
exercises.purpose: Improve walking speed, endurance, and quality
exercises.modifications: Progress from parallel bars to assistive devices to independent. Increase speed and distance progressively.

exercises.name: Cardiovascular Exercise
exercises.purpose: Improve cardiorespiratory fitness, reduce cardiovascular risk
exercises.modifications: Treadmill (with harness if needed), cycling, recumbent stepper

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training - Lower Limb
exercises.purpose: Improve strength, power, functional mobility
exercises.modifications: Focus on affected side but train bilaterally. Include hip, knee, ankle.

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training - Upper Limb
exercises.purpose: Improve arm function, ADL capacity
exercises.modifications: Bilateral training, functional reaching tasks, constraint-induced therapy principles

exercises.name: Balance and Coordination Training
exercises.purpose: Reduce falls risk, improve dynamic stability
exercises.modifications: Static to dynamic, narrow base, perturbations, dual-task

exercises.name: Flexibility and Range of Motion
exercises.dosage: Daily, 30 sec holds, major muscle groups
exercises.purpose: Prevent contractures, maintain mobility
exercises.modifications: Focus on affected side, particularly ankle plantarflexors, hip flexors
exercises.evidence_level: Low quality evidence but clinically important

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Ongoing - significant improvements continue up to 12+ months post-stroke. Early intensive rehabilitation critical.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Higher dose exercise (>20 hours in first 3 months) associated with better outcomes. Task-specific training superior to non-specific exercise. Cardiovascular training improves fitness and functional capacity. Combination of aerobic, strength, and balance training recommended.

phases.goals: Early mobilization, prevent complications, optimize medical management
phases.criteria: Medical stability required. Progress from sitting to standing to walking with assistance.

phases.goals: Maximize functional recovery, intensive task-specific training
phases.criteria: Intensive therapy 4-5x/week. Rapid functional gains expected. Progress intensity and complexity.

phases.goals: Optimize function, maintain fitness, secondary prevention
phases.criteria: Continued improvements possible beyond 6 months. Focus on maintenance and community participation.

progression.evidence_base: Early and intensive rehabilitation supported by extensive evidence. Neuroplasticity continues beyond traditional windows.

absolute: Recent hemorrhagic stroke (timing depends on medical advice)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Most rapid recovery in first 3 months, continued improvements up to 12+ months with ongoing intervention
key_outcomes: Improved gait speed (0.1-0.2 m/s improvement clinically meaningful)
outcomes.effect_sizes: Gait speed: SMD 0.32-0.52 depending on intervention. Balance: SMD 0.43 (95% CI 0.26-0.60). Fitness: VO2peak increase 15-20%.

key_findings: Higher dose exercise associated with better functional outcomes
Task-specific training superior to general exercise
Cardiovascular fitness training safe and effective
Combined interventions superior to single modality
Exercise effective in chronic phase (>6 months)
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Gait speed: MD 0.13 m/s (0.09-0.17). Balance (Berg): MD 3.74 points (2.17-5.32). ADL independence: OR 1.82 (1.4-2.37).
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from numerous RCTs and systematic reviews

references.citation: Saunders DH, et al. Physical fitness training for stroke patients. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2020;3:CD003316
references.key_finding: Cardiorespiratory training improves fitness and functional capacity post-stroke

references.citation: Stroke Foundation. Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management. Melbourne, Australia. 2023
references.key_finding: Comprehensive Australian guidelines recommend early intensive task-specific rehabilitation

references.citation: English C, et al. Circuit class therapy for improving mobility after stroke. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2017
references.key_finding: Circuit class training effective and efficient for improving walking capacity

clinical_note: Screen for depression and fatigue - common post-stroke. BP monitoring essential during exercise. Fall risk high - ensure safe environment. Encourage family/carer involvement. Multidisciplinary approach critical (PT, OT, SLP, Psychologist, EP). Secondary prevention paramount (BP control, statins, antiplatelet therapy, lifestyle modification).

overview.pathophysiology: Mood disorder characterized by persistent low mood, anhedonia, and cognitive/behavioral symptoms. Involves dysregulation of neurotransmitters (serotonin, dopamine, norepinephrine), HPA axis dysfunction, and inflammatory processes.
overview.functional_impact: Reduced motivation, fatigue, cognitive impairment, social withdrawal, reduced physical activity, sleep disturbance, reduced quality of life. Impacts occupational and social functioning.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~1 in 7 Australians in their lifetime. Major cause of disability. Higher rates in females, young adults, and those with chronic health conditions.

assessment.evidence_base: Comprehensive assessment recommended by ESSA Depression Position Statement and NICE guidelines

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise - Moderate Intensity
exercises.purpose: Reduce depressive symptoms, improve mood, enhance neuroplasticity
exercises.modifications: Patient preference important - walking, cycling, swimming, group classes
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - effect size comparable to psychotherapy

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise - Vigorous Intensity
exercises.purpose: Greater antidepressant effects than moderate intensity
exercises.modifications: For those with baseline fitness - running, interval training
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate to high quality evidence - dose-response relationship

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 8-12 reps, major muscle groups, 2-3x/week
exercises.purpose: Reduce depressive symptoms, improve self-efficacy, functional capacity
exercises.modifications: Can be standalone or combined with aerobic. Progressive overload important.
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence - effective as standalone intervention

exercises.name: Mind-Body Exercise
exercises.purpose: Reduce symptoms, mindfulness, stress reduction, flexibility
exercises.modifications: Group classes beneficial for social connection

exercises.name: Group-Based Exercise
exercises.dosage: As above but in supervised group
exercises.purpose: Social support, adherence, additional mental health benefits
exercises.modifications: Consider group preference - may enhance outcomes
exercises.evidence_level: Social component adds benefits beyond exercise alone

exercise_prescription.frequency: 3-5 sessions per week - consistency more important than intensity initially
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 8-12 weeks for antidepressant effects. Ongoing maintenance essential to prevent relapse.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise has moderate to large antidepressant effects comparable to psychotherapy and pharmacotherapy. Dose-response relationship exists (higher volume associated with greater effects). Effects maintained with continued exercise but diminish with cessation. Mechanisms include neuroplasticity, inflammation reduction, HPA axis regulation, self-efficacy.

phases.goals: Establish exercise habit, build confidence, identify preferences
phases.criteria: Low to moderate intensity, focus on enjoyment and feasibility. Success = attendance and completion.

phases.goals: Build fitness, achieve recommended volumes, symptom reduction
phases.criteria: Gradually increase to guideline levels. Progress when consistently attending and tolerating exercise well.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise behavior, prevent relapse, integrate into lifestyle
phases.criteria: Transition to independent or group-based community exercise. Ongoing support as needed.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach with emphasis on adherence and enjoyment supported by behavioral literature

absolute: Acute suicidal ideation with plan and intent (requires immediate psychiatric care)
relative: Severe depression with psychomotor retardation (may need lower intensity initially)
Medication side effects affecting exercise safety

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Initial mood improvements within 2-3 weeks. Clinically significant reduction in depressive symptoms by 8-12 weeks.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Depression severity: SMD -0.62 to -0.72 (moderate to large). Effect sizes larger with higher intensity/volume and in those with diagnosed depression vs. symptoms only.

key_findings: Exercise has moderate to large antidepressant effects
Comparable to psychotherapy and antidepressants
Both aerobic and resistance training effective
Higher intensity associated with larger effects
Supervised programs superior to unsupervised
Effects diminish after exercise cessation
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Depression severity: SMD -0.72 (95% CI -0.91 to -0.53) for supervised exercise. Moderate intensity: -0.62. Vigorous intensity: -0.73.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence from numerous RCTs and meta-analyses. Some heterogeneity in populations and interventions.

references.citation: Schuch FB, et al. Exercise as a treatment for depression: a meta-analysis. J Psychiatr Res. 2016;77:42-51
references.key_finding: Exercise has large antidepressant effects, especially for those with diagnosed depression

references.citation: Kvam S, et al. Exercise as a treatment for depression: a meta-analysis. J Affect Disord. 2016;202:67-86
references.key_finding: Exercise effects comparable to psychotherapy and antidepressants

references.citation: ESSA Position Statement: Exercise and Mental Health. 2021
references.key_finding: Exercise Physiologists have key role in mental health care through exercise prescription

clinical_note: Screen for suicide risk regularly - refer immediately if present. Exercise adjunct to, not replacement for, psychological/pharmacological treatment in moderate-severe depression. Address barriers to exercise participation. Emphasize achievable goals and self-compassion. Consider referral to psychologist if indicated. Collaborate with GP/psychiatrist.

overview.pathophysiology: Chronic pain syndrome characterized by widespread musculoskeletal pain, central sensitization, altered pain processing. Involves neurotransmitter dysregulation, HPA axis dysfunction, sleep disturbance.
overview.functional_impact: Widespread pain, fatigue, sleep disturbance, cognitive dysfunction ('fibro fog'), mood disturbance, severe disability, reduced quality of life.
overview.prevalence: Affects 2-4% of Australians. More common in females (7:1 ratio). Peak age 40-55 years.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per ACR 2016 criteria and fibromyalgia guidelines

exercises.name: Low-Moderate Aerobic Exercise
exercises.dosage: 20-30 minutes at low-moderate intensity, 2-3x/week, VERY gradual progression
exercises.purpose: Reduce pain and fatigue, improve function - CORNERSTONE
exercises.modifications: Walking, cycling, swimming, aquatic exercise. START VERY LOW. Expect initial pain increase.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - most effective intervention

exercises.name: Aquatic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Exercise with less impact, warm water beneficial for pain
exercises.modifications: Warm pool preferred. Gentle movements.

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 1-2 sets of 10-15 reps at LOW intensity, 2x/week
exercises.purpose: Improve strength, function (BUT aerobic more effective)
exercises.modifications: Very light resistance initially. Progress extremely slowly.

exercises.name: Flexibility
exercises.purpose: Maintain ROM, reduce stiffness
exercises.modifications: Gentle, no bouncing

exercises.name: Tai Chi / Qigong
exercises.purpose: Combine gentle movement, mindfulness, relaxation
exercises.modifications: Well-suited for fibromyalgia

exercise_prescription.frequency: 2-4 sessions per week initially - frequency more important than intensity
exercise_prescription.program_duration: CRITICAL: Very gradual progression over 12-24 weeks. Ongoing long-term essential.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise is most effective non-pharmaceutical treatment for fibromyalgia. Aerobic exercise most beneficial - reduces pain, fatigue, improves function and mood. CRITICAL: Must start very low and progress extremely slowly - initial symptom exacerbation common and major barrier. Aquatic exercise particularly well-tolerated. Benefits maintained only with continued exercise. Graded approach essential - too much too fast causes dropout.

phases.goals: Build tolerance, manage initial flare, establish habit
phases.criteria: START VERY LOW (5-10 min). Expect temporary pain increase. Education critical. Progress when tolerating baseline.

phases.goals: Slowly build to recommended levels
phases.criteria: Increase duration by 10% every 1-2 weeks ONLY if tolerated. Some setbacks expected and normal.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise levels, manage symptoms
phases.criteria: Minimum 2-3x/week. Benefits lost when stopping.

progression.evidence_base: Very gradual progression critical per fibromyalgia exercise research

relative: Severe pain flare (may need temporary reduction)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Initial worsening possible first 2-4 weeks. Improvements typically 8-16 weeks.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Pain: SMD -0.65. Physical function: SMD 0.66. Well-being: SMD 0.49. Large effects.

key_findings: Aerobic exercise reduces pain and improves function in fibromyalgia
Aquatic exercise particularly beneficial
Very gradual progression essential
Initial symptom exacerbation common - education critical
Long-term adherence required for sustained benefits
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Health-related QoL: SMD 0.49 (0.23-0.75). Physical function: SMD 0.66. Pain: SMD -0.65. Large beneficial effects.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence from multiple RCTs

references.citation: Busch AJ, et al. Exercise for fibromyalgia: a systematic review. J Rheumatol. 2008
references.key_finding: Aerobic exercise beneficial for fibromyalgia symptoms

references.citation: Bidonde J, et al. Aquatic exercise training for fibromyalgia. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014
references.key_finding: Aquatic exercise improves wellness, symptoms, and fitness in fibromyalgia

references.citation: Macfarlane GJ, et al. EULAR revised recommendations for the management of fibromyalgia. Ann Rheum Dis. 2017
references.key_finding: Exercise strongly recommended as core treatment

clinical_note: CRITICAL: 'Start low, go slow' - most important principle. Initial pain increase expected and normal - prepare patients or they will dropout. Emphasize 'graded exercise' not 'graded activity pacing'. Address catastrophizing and fear-avoidance. Pain education essential. Often comorbid with depression, sleep disorders - address concurrently. Multidisciplinary approach ideal (psychologist, sleep medicine). Many will say 'exercise makes me worse' - this reflects poor previous exercise prescription (too much too fast).

overview.pathophysiology: Chronic inflammatory arthritis primarily affecting axial skeleton (spine, sacroiliac joints). Progressive ankylosis, spinal fusion, loss of mobility. HLA-B27 associated.
overview.functional_impact: Back pain, stiffness (especially morning), reduced spinal mobility, kyphosis, reduced chest expansion, peripheral arthritis, enthesitis, fatigue, functional limitations.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~0.5-1% of Australians. More common in males (3:1). Peak onset 20-30 years.

key_assessments: Spinal ROM (modified Schober's, lateral flexion, rotation)
assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per ASAS and EULAR AS guidelines

exercises.name: Spinal Extension and Mobility Exercises
exercises.purpose: Maintain spinal mobility, prevent kyphosis - MOST CRITICAL
exercises.modifications: Extension exercises, rotation, lateral flexion. Wall stands, prone extensions.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - cornerstone of management

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Reduce inflammation, improve fitness, manage fatigue
exercises.modifications: Swimming especially beneficial. Avoid high-impact if peripheral involvement.

exercises.name: Postural Training
exercises.purpose: Prevent kyphotic posture
exercises.modifications: Mirror feedback, postural awareness exercises

exercises.name: Breathing Exercises
exercises.purpose: Maintain chest expansion, prevent restrictive lung disease
exercises.modifications: Deep breathing, chest expansion exercises

exercises.name: Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Support posture, improve function
exercises.modifications: Focus on back extensors, scapular muscles

exercise_prescription.frequency: Daily spinal exercises ESSENTIAL; additional exercise 3-5x/week
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Lifelong daily exercise CRITICAL
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise is most important non-pharmacological intervention in AS. Daily spinal extension/mobility exercises CRITICAL to prevent ankylosis and kyphosis. Strong evidence for improving spinal mobility, function, pain, quality of life. MUST be lifelong - benefits lost when stopped. Combination of home exercises and supervised programs optimal.

phases.goals: Establish daily routine, learn proper technique
phases.criteria: Start gently. Education on importance of daily exercise. Build habit.

phases.goals: Optimize mobility, build endurance
phases.criteria: Progress exercises. Expect improvements in mobility and pain.

phases.goals: Prevent ankylosis, maintain mobility
phases.criteria: DAILY spinal exercises non-negotiable. Regular aerobic activity.

progression.evidence_base: Daily exercise essential per ASAS/EULAR guidelines - strongest recommendation

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Mobility and pain improvements within 6-12 weeks
key_outcomes: Improved function and quality of life
outcomes.effect_sizes: Spinal mobility: moderate to large effects. Pain: moderate effects. Function: moderate to large effects.

key_findings: Exercise essential in AS management
Improves spinal mobility, pain, function
Prevents ankylosis and kyphosis
Supervised programs plus home exercise optimal
Must be lifelong - benefits lost when stopped
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: BASMI: significant improvements. BASDAI: moderate reductions. BASFI: moderate to large improvements.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from multiple RCTs and Cochrane reviews

references.citation: Dagfinrud H, et al. Physiotherapy interventions for ankylosing spondylitis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2008
references.key_finding: Exercise effective for AS - improves function and mobility

references.citation: van der Heijde D, et al. 2016 update of the ASAS-EULAR management recommendations for axial spondyloarthritis. Ann Rheum Dis. 2017
references.key_finding: Exercise strongly recommended as core treatment

clinical_note: Daily spinal extension/mobility exercises are NON-NEGOTIABLE - most important intervention to prevent ankylosis. 'Use it or lose it' - stopped exercise leads to rapid mobility loss. Supervised programs beneficial but home program essential. NSAIDs + biologics important for inflammation control. Smoking cessation critical (accelerates disease). Postural awareness constant. Swimming ideal exercise. Morning stiffness managed with hot shower then exercises. Lifelong commitment required.

overview.pathophysiology: Progressive shoulder stiffness and pain from capsular inflammation and fibrosis. Three phases: freezing (painful), frozen (stiff), thawing (recovery). Often idiopathic or secondary to diabetes, trauma.
overview.functional_impact: Severe shoulder pain (especially night pain), progressive ROM loss (external rotation most limited), functional disability, ADL limitations, sleep disturbance.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~2-5% of general population. More common in females. Age 40-65 years. Higher incidence in diabetes (~10-36%).

key_assessments: Active and passive ROM (flexion, abduction, internal/external rotation)
screening_tools: Red flag screening for alternative diagnoses
assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per shoulder disorder clinical guidelines

exercises.name: Gentle ROM Exercises
exercises.dosage: Multiple times daily, within pain tolerance
exercises.purpose: Restore ROM, prevent further stiffness - PRIMARY GOAL
exercises.modifications: Pendulum exercises, wall walks, pulley exercises. Progress based on phase and pain.
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence - cornerstone but variable efficacy

exercises.name: Capsular Stretching
exercises.purpose: Address capsular restriction
exercises.modifications: Gentle sustained holds. May be more effective in thawing phase.

exercises.name: Scapular Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Improve scapulohumeral rhythm, support shoulder function
exercises.modifications: Start once pain controlled. Progress gradually.

exercises.name: Functional Activities
exercises.purpose: Restore ADL independence
exercises.modifications: Progress as ROM improves

exercise_prescription.frequency: Multiple times daily for ROM exercises; 3-5x/week for strengthening
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Natural history 1-3 years. Exercise throughout to optimize recovery.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise has modest benefits - not curative but important to optimize outcomes. Most effective in thawing phase. Gentle ROM exercises help restore function. Avoid aggressive stretching in freezing phase (may worsen pain). Manual therapy + exercise may be more effective than exercise alone. Corticosteroid injections often needed for pain control. Natural resolution common but takes 1-3 years - exercise may accelerate.

phases.goals: Pain management, prevent excessive ROM loss
phases.criteria: GENTLE ROM exercises only. Avoid aggravating. Pain control priority.

phases.goals: Maintain ROM, gentle stretching
phases.criteria: Continue ROM exercises. May intensify stretching if pain allows.

phases.goals: Maximize ROM recovery, restore function
phases.criteria: Aggressive stretching appropriate. Strengthening. Functional restoration.

progression.evidence_base: Phase-specific approach based on natural history

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Variable - natural history 1-3 years total. Exercise may accelerate recovery by several months.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Modest effects - exercise alone not curative but beneficial adjunct

key_findings: Exercise provides modest benefits
Most effective in thawing phase
Combination with manual therapy may be superior
Corticosteroid injections often needed
Natural resolution common - exercise may accelerate
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Small to moderate effect sizes for pain and function
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate quality evidence from limited RCTs

references.citation: Kelley MJ, et al. Shoulder pain and mobility deficits: adhesive capsulitis. J Orthop Sports Phys Ther. 2013
references.key_finding: Clinical practice guidelines for frozen shoulder

references.citation: Page MJ, et al. Manual therapy and exercise for adhesive capsulitis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014
references.key_finding: Moderate evidence for manual therapy + exercise

clinical_note: Natural history: eventual recovery in most (1-3 years) - set realistic expectations. Exercise has modest benefits but important to optimize recovery. Avoid aggressive stretching in freezing phase. Corticosteroid injection + exercise often more effective than exercise alone. Higher incidence in diabetes - glycemic control important. Hydrodilatation or manipulation under anesthesia for refractory cases. Night pain characteristic - manage with medications/injections. Patient education on natural history crucial. Some residual stiffness common (~10-15° external rotation loss).

overview.pathophysiology: Chronic tendinopathy of wrist extensor origin at lateral epicondyle. Degenerative changes, failed healing, angiofibroblastic hyperplasia. Not inflammatory. Overuse related.
overview.functional_impact: Lateral elbow pain, grip weakness, difficulty with gripping/lifting activities, functional limitations in work/sport/ADLs.
overview.prevalence: Affects 1-3% of general population. Peak age 40-50 years. Occupational and sports-related risk factors.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per tendinopathy clinical guidelines

exercises.name: Eccentric Wrist Extension Exercises
exercises.purpose: Tendon remodeling, load tolerance - MOST EFFECTIVE
exercises.modifications: Tyler Twist with Theraband or similar. Slow controlled eccentric phase.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - most effective intervention

exercises.name: Isometric Exercises
exercises.purpose: Pain relief, tendon conditioning
exercises.modifications: Low-load, pain-free. Useful early in program.

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Build load capacity, return to function
exercises.modifications: Progress from eccentric to concentric-eccentric. Functional activities.

exercises.name: Grip Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Restore grip strength and function
exercises.modifications: Graded progression

exercise_prescription.program_duration: 12-16 weeks typically. May need longer (up to 6-12 months).
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise, particularly eccentric training, is most effective treatment for lateral epicondylalgia. Superior to wait-and-see, comparable to corticosteroid injections short-term, superior long-term. Load management critical. Gradual progressive loading essential. High recurrence rate if inadequate loading/rehabilitation. Other treatments (injections, bracing, manual therapy) provide short-term relief but exercise provides long-term cure.

phases.goals: Reduce pain, begin isometric exercises
phases.criteria: Pain-free isometrics. Activity modification. Load management.

phases.goals: Tendon remodeling through eccentric loading
phases.criteria: Daily eccentric exercises. Gradual load increases. Some discomfort acceptable.

phases.goals: Build capacity, return to function
phases.criteria: Heavier loads. Functional activities. Return to sport/work.

phases.goals: Prevent recurrence
phases.criteria: Continued strengthening. Load management.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive loading approach per tendinopathy guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvements typically 8-12 weeks. May take 6-12 months for full resolution.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Large effects for pain and function with eccentric training

key_findings: Eccentric exercise most effective treatment
Superior to wait-and-see, comparable to injections short-term, superior long-term
Load management critical
Other interventions provide short-term relief only
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Large effect sizes for eccentric training on pain and function
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence from multiple RCTs

references.citation: Coombes BK, et al. Efficacy and safety of corticosteroid injections and other injections for management of tendinopathy: a systematic review. Lancet. 2010
references.key_finding: Corticosteroids provide short-term relief but poor long-term outcomes

references.citation: Cullinane FL, et al. Acute injuries to the extensor mechanism of the knee. J Bone Joint Surg Am. 2010
references.key_finding: Exercise therapy effective for tendinopathy

clinical_note: Load management crucial - often 'too much too soon' precipitates. Eccentric exercises may cause discomfort - educate that some pain acceptable. Corticosteroid injections provide short-term relief but associated with higher recurrence - not first-line. Avoid injections if possible. Bracing may help symptomatically but not curative. Workplace/activity modification important. High recurrence if inadequate rehabilitation. Surgery rarely needed (<5% cases) - reserve for refractory cases after 6-12 months conservative management. PRP/other injections: limited evidence.

overview.pathophysiology: Anterior knee pain from patellofemoral joint dysfunction. Multifactorial: biomechanical, neuromuscular, anatomical factors. Increased patellofemoral stress, altered tracking, muscle imbalances.
overview.functional_impact: Anterior knee pain with activities (stairs, squatting, running, prolonged sitting), functional limitations, reduced sports/exercise participation.
overview.prevalence: Very common, especially in young active adults and adolescents. Female predominance (2:1). Peak age 12-35 years.

key_assessments: Pain provocation tests (squat, stairs, patellar compression)
Hip and knee strength testing (especially hip abductors, external rotators, quadriceps)
screening_tools: Red flag screening for other pathology
assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per patellofemoral pain clinical guidelines

exercises.name: Hip Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Improve hip control, reduce dynamic knee valgus - CRITICAL
exercises.modifications: Hip abduction, external rotation, extension exercises. Clamshells, side-lying abduction, monster walks.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - most important intervention

exercises.name: Quadriceps Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Improve quadriceps strength and VMO timing
exercises.modifications: Terminal knee extension, leg press, step-ups. Avoid deep knee flexion initially.

exercises.name: Functional Training
exercises.purpose: Improve movement quality, reduce aberrant mechanics
exercises.modifications: Single leg squat, step downs, running mechanics. Focus on control.

exercises.name: Neuromuscular Training
exercises.purpose: Improve dynamic stability and movement patterns
exercises.modifications: Balance exercises, perturbation training, mirror feedback

exercise_prescription.program_duration: 6-12 weeks typically. May need longer in chronic cases.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise is most effective treatment for patellofemoral pain. Hip strengthening particularly important - reduces knee valgus and PFJ stress. Combination of hip and knee strengthening superior to knee alone. Functional training essential. Taping and orthotics provide short-term symptom relief but exercise addresses underlying issues. High-quality evidence for exercise reducing pain and improving function.

phases.goals: Pain control, begin strengthening, activity modification
phases.criteria: Simple strengthening exercises. Avoid pain-provoking activities.

phases.goals: Build hip and knee strength, improve control
phases.criteria: Progressive resistance training. Emphasis on hip abductors/external rotators.

phases.goals: Improve movement quality, return to activity
phases.criteria: Functional exercises. Sport-specific training. Gradual return to running/sport.

phases.goals: Prevent recurrence
phases.criteria: Continued strengthening 2-3x/week.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive functional approach per PFP guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Pain reduction within 6-8 weeks. Functional improvements by 12 weeks.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Moderate to large effects for pain and function

key_findings: Exercise most effective treatment for PFP
Hip strengthening particularly important
Hip + knee strengthening superior to knee alone
Functional training essential
Good prognosis with appropriate rehabilitation
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Pain: moderate to large reductions. Function: moderate to large improvements.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and systematic reviews

references.citation: Crossley KM, et al. 2016 Patellofemoral pain consensus statement. Br J Sports Med. 2016
references.key_finding: International consensus on PFP management - exercise cornerstone

references.citation: Collins NJ, et al. Prognostic factors for patellofemoral pain: a multicentre observational analysis. Br J Sports Med. 2013
references.key_finding: Hip weakness key factor in PFP

clinical_note: Hip strengthening is CRITICAL - often neglected but most important. Address biomechanics - dynamic knee valgus common culprit. Patellar taping may provide short-term relief - useful adjunct. Foot orthotics controversial - may help some but not curative. Activity modification important early - avoid stairs, deep squats, prolonged sitting. Gradual return to running with good mechanics. High recurrence if inadequate rehabilitation. Most recover well with appropriate exercise. Adolescent females high-risk group. 'Runner's knee' common term.

overview.pathophysiology: Progressive neurodegenerative disorder with loss of dopaminergic neurons in substantia nigra. Results in motor symptoms (tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, postural instability) and non-motor symptoms.
overview.functional_impact: Tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, postural instability, freezing of gait, falls, reduced balance, impaired ADLs, depression, cognitive impairment, reduced quality of life.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~80,000 Australians. Second most common neurodegenerative disease. Prevalence increases with age. More common in males.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per Parkinson's disease clinical guidelines and Movement Disorder Society

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Improve fitness, mobility, cognition, mood - NEUROPROTECTIVE
exercises.modifications: Treadmill, cycling, boxing programs. Higher intensity beneficial.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - may slow disease progression

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Combat muscle weakness, improve function
exercises.modifications: Major muscle groups. Functional movements.

exercises.name: Balance and Gait Training
exercises.purpose: Reduce falls risk, improve stability and walking - CRITICAL
exercises.modifications: Cueing strategies, dual-task training, turning practice, obstacle courses

exercises.name: Flexibility and Stretching
exercises.dosage: Daily, focus on commonly rigid areas
exercises.purpose: Combat rigidity, maintain ROM
exercises.modifications: Sustained stretches for neck, trunk, hips

exercises.name: Dance/Rhythmic Movement
exercises.purpose: Combine exercise with rhythm, cueing, social engagement
exercises.modifications: Tango, ballroom, or other dance forms effective

exercises.name: Boxing Training
exercises.purpose: Combines aerobic, strength, balance, cognitive demands
exercises.modifications: Rock Steady Boxing or similar programs

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Ongoing lifelong intervention - consistency critical
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise is essential disease-modifying intervention in PD. High-quality evidence for reducing motor symptoms, improving balance, reducing falls, enhancing quality of life. Emerging evidence for neuroprotective effects and slowing disease progression. Higher-intensity exercise appears more beneficial. Multimodal programs combining aerobic, strength, balance most effective. Cueing strategies critical for gait/freezing.

phases.goals: Establish safety, build tolerance, learn cueing strategies
phases.criteria: Start conservatively. Assess response. Education on exercise benefits.

phases.goals: Build toward high-intensity multimodal program
phases.criteria: Progress intensity and complexity. Emphasize balance work.

phases.goals: Sustain high exercise levels, manage symptoms
phases.criteria: Minimum 5 days/week activity. Ongoing challenging program essential.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive multimodal approach per PD exercise guidelines

relative: Severe dyskinesias (may need medication timing adjustment)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvements in motor function within 8-12 weeks
key_outcomes: Better balance and reduced falls (30-60% reduction)
outcomes.effect_sizes: Motor function: SMD 0.37. Balance: large effects. Falls: RR 0.47. Quality of life: moderate to large effects.

key_findings: Exercise improves motor function, balance, gait in PD
Reduces falls by ~50%
High-intensity exercise appears superior
May have neuroprotective effects
Benefits across all disease stages
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Motor UPDRS: MD -5.48 points. Gait speed: MD +0.06 m/s. Falls: RR 0.47. Quality of life: significant improvements.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs

references.citation: Tomlinson CL, et al. Physiotherapy for Parkinson's disease: a comparison of techniques. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014
references.key_finding: Physiotherapy improves motor function and quality of life in PD

references.citation: Allen NE, et al. Balance and falls in Parkinson's disease: a meta-analysis. Mov Disord. 2011
references.key_finding: Exercise reduces falls in PD by 50%

references.citation: Uhrbrand A, et al. Parkinson's disease and intensive exercise therapy - a systematic review. Dan Med J. 2015
references.key_finding: High-intensity exercise beneficial in PD

clinical_note: Exercise is disease-modifying intervention - not just symptom management. Higher intensity appears more beneficial. 'ON' medication timing often optimal for exercise. Address freezing of gait with cueing (visual, auditory, tactile). Falls prevention critical - multifactorial (balance training, strength, medication review, home safety). Depression common - exercise helps. Cognitive decline - exercise beneficial. Multidisciplinary care essential. Exercise complements medication - doesn't replace it.

overview.pathophysiology: Interruption of blood supply to brain causing neuronal death. Results in various neurological deficits depending on location and extent. Recovery involves neuroplasticity and compensation.
overview.functional_impact: Hemiparesis/hemiplegia, reduced balance, altered gait, reduced endurance, spasticity, reduced ADL independence, communication difficulties, cognitive impairment.
overview.prevalence: ~56,000 strokes annually in Australia. Leading cause of disability. Ischaemic strokes comprise ~85% of all strokes.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per stroke rehabilitation guidelines and National Stroke Foundation

exercises.name: Task-Specific Gait Training
exercises.purpose: Improve walking ability, speed, endurance - PRIMARY GOAL
exercises.modifications: Body-weight supported treadmill, overground, stairs. High repetition essential.

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Improve paretic limb strength - essential for function
exercises.modifications: Both affected and unaffected sides. Functional movements.

exercises.name: Cardiovascular Fitness Training
exercises.purpose: Improve endurance, reduce cardiovascular risk
exercises.modifications: Cycling, treadmill, seated exercises if needed

exercises.name: Balance Training
exercises.purpose: Reduce falls risk, improve stability
exercises.modifications: Static and dynamic balance activities. Progressive challenge.

exercises.name: Upper Limb Task Practice
exercises.purpose: Improve arm function and ADL independence
exercises.modifications: Constraint-induced movement therapy if appropriate. High repetition.

exercise_prescription.frequency: 5-7 days per week if possible - intensity and repetition critical
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Intensive early rehabilitation (first 6 months). Ongoing for continued gains.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise and physical rehabilitation are cornerstone of stroke recovery. Task-specific high-repetition training most effective. Earlier and more intensive therapy associated with better outcomes. Strength training improves function without increasing spasticity. Cardiovascular fitness training safe and beneficial. Neuroplasticity greatest in first 6 months but continues long-term.

phases.goals: Early mobilization, prevent complications
phases.criteria: Medically stable. Gentle mobilization. Screen for complications.

phases.goals: Maximize recovery, intensive task-specific training
phases.criteria: Intensive therapy 3-5 hours/day if tolerated. High repetition. Neuroplasticity window.

phases.goals: Continue functional gains, prepare for community reintegration
phases.criteria: Continued intensive therapy. Functional activities. Community access.

phases.goals: Maintain gains, ongoing improvements possible, secondary prevention
phases.criteria: Ongoing exercise 3-5x/week. Continued gains possible with sustained effort.

progression.evidence_base: Time-dependent recovery with intensive early intervention per stroke guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Greatest recovery first 3-6 months. Continued gains possible long-term with continued training.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Gait training: large effects on walking. Strength training: moderate effects on function. Overall: significant functional gains with intensive rehabilitation.

key_findings: Intensive task-specific training improves outcomes
Earlier and more intensive therapy associated with better recovery
Strength training improves function without increasing spasticity
Cardiovascular training safe and beneficial
Benefits continue in chronic phase with continued training
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Gait training improves walking speed and independence. Strength training improves function. Moderate to large effect sizes.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and Cochrane reviews

references.citation: Pollock A, et al. Physical rehabilitation approaches for the recovery of function and mobility following stroke. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2014
references.key_finding: Physical rehabilitation improves function and mobility post-stroke

references.citation: National Stroke Foundation Australia. Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management. 2017
references.key_finding: Australian evidence-based stroke rehabilitation guidelines

references.citation: Veerbeek JM, et al. What is the evidence for physical therapy poststroke? A systematic review and meta-analysis. PLoS One. 2014
references.key_finding: Comprehensive review of PT interventions post-stroke

clinical_note: Time is critical - intensive early rehabilitation within first 6 months optimal. Task-specific high-repetition training most effective. Neuroplasticity continues long-term - chronic stroke patients can still improve. Strength training does NOT increase spasticity - outdated concern. Address secondary prevention (BP, lipids, diabetes, smoking). Depression common - screen and treat. Constraint-induced movement therapy effective for appropriate candidates. Multidisciplinary approach essential. Family education and involvement critical.

overview.pathophysiology: Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease - progressive airflow limitation from emphysema and/or chronic bronchitis. Airway inflammation, mucus hypersecretion, alveolar destruction.
overview.functional_impact: Dyspnea (breathlessness), reduced exercise tolerance, fatigue, frequent exacerbations, impaired quality of life, progressive disability, high mortality.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~7-8% of Australian adults >40 years. Fourth leading cause of death globally. Major cause of disability.

key_assessments: Modified Medical Research Council (mMRC) dyspnea scale
assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per COPD-X Australian guidelines and GOLD criteria

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise Training
exercises.purpose: Improve exercise capacity, reduce dyspnea, enhance quality of life - CORNERSTONE
exercises.modifications: Walking, cycling most common. Interval training if continuous not tolerated.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - most important component

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Combat muscle weakness, improve functional capacity
exercises.modifications: Upper and lower body. Essential component.

exercises.name: Inspiratory Muscle Training
exercises.dosage: 30 minutes daily at 30% maximal inspiratory pressure
exercises.purpose: Improve respiratory muscle strength and dyspnea
exercises.modifications: Threshold or resistance device

exercises.name: Flexibility
exercises.dosage: Daily, focus on chest wall and shoulders
exercises.purpose: Maintain mobility, complement exercise program
exercises.modifications: Gentle sustained stretches

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Pulmonary rehabilitation 6-12 weeks. Ongoing maintenance essential - benefits lost when stopping.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Pulmonary rehabilitation (exercise + education) is most effective intervention in COPD. Reduces dyspnea, improves exercise capacity, enhances quality of life, reduces hospital admissions. Benefits moderate to large and clinically meaningful. Both aerobic and resistance training essential. Benefits decline when exercise stopped - ongoing maintenance critical.

phases.goals: Improve exercise tolerance, education, symptom management
phases.criteria: Supervised program 2-3x/week. Progressive overload. Oxygen if needed.

phases.goals: Prepare for independent exercise, consolidate gains
phases.criteria: Mix of supervised and independent. Self-management focus.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise levels, prevent decline
phases.criteria: Minimum 3x/week exercise. Community programs. Benefits lost if stopped.

progression.evidence_base: Structured pulmonary rehabilitation followed by maintenance per COPD-X guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvements typically within 6-8 weeks of pulmonary rehabilitation
outcomes.effect_sizes: 6MWT: MD +43.93m. Quality of life: standardized improvements 0.5-1.0 (moderate to large). Hospital admissions reduced.

key_findings: Pulmonary rehabilitation highly effective in COPD
Improves exercise capacity, dyspnea, quality of life
Reduces hospital admissions
Both aerobic and resistance training essential
Benefits decline when exercise stopped
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: 6-Minute Walk: MD +43.93m (34.82 to 53.04). CRQ dyspnea: MD 0.79 (0.56-1.03). CRQ fatigue: MD 0.68. Hospital admissions reduced.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from Cochrane reviews and extensive RCTs

references.citation: McCarthy B, et al. Pulmonary rehabilitation for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015
references.key_finding: Pulmonary rehab improves quality of life, exercise capacity, reduces hospital admissions

references.citation: COPD-X Australian and New Zealand Guidelines. 2024
references.key_finding: Australian evidence-based COPD management guidelines

references.citation: Spruit MA, et al. An official American Thoracic Society/European Respiratory Society statement: key concepts in pulmonary rehabilitation. Am J Respir Crit Care Med. 2013
references.key_finding: International pulmonary rehabilitation standards

clinical_note: Pulmonary rehabilitation significantly underutilized despite strong evidence. Exercise is safe even in severe COPD. Supplemental oxygen if SpO2 <88% during exercise. Smoking cessation absolutely critical - refer to Quitline. Address nutrition (cachexia or obesity common). Exacerbation management plan essential. Medications optimize but exercise provides additional critical benefits. Screen for anxiety/depression - very common. Maintenance exercise essential - benefits lost within months if stopped. Community maintenance programs ideal.

overview.pathophysiology: Mood disorder with persistent low mood, anhedonia, neurobiological changes (monoamine deficiency, HPA axis dysregulation, inflammation, neuroplasticity impairment).
overview.functional_impact: Low mood, anhedonia, fatigue, poor concentration, sleep disturbance, reduced motivation, social withdrawal, functional impairment, increased physical health risks.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~10-15% of Australians in their lifetime. Leading cause of disability worldwide. Moderate severity common presentation.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per mental health treatment guidelines and DSM-5 criteria

exercises.name: Moderate-Intensity Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Reduce depressive symptoms - HIGHLY EFFECTIVE
exercises.modifications: Walking, jogging, cycling, swimming. Group classes beneficial for social aspect.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - comparable to antidepressants

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Reduce depression, improve self-efficacy and body image
exercises.modifications: Major muscle groups. Circuit training effective.

exercises.name: Yoga
exercises.purpose: Reduce depression, anxiety, improve mindfulness
exercises.modifications: Various styles effective

exercises.name: Group Exercise Classes
exercises.purpose: Combine exercise benefits with social connection
exercises.modifications: Any group format - social aspect therapeutic

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 8-12 weeks for therapeutic effect. Ongoing for maintenance.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise is highly effective treatment for depression - comparable to psychotherapy and antidepressants. Effects moderate to large. Aerobic exercise most studied. Resistance training also effective. Mechanisms: neurobiological (BDNF, monoamines, inflammation), psychological (self-efficacy, mastery, distraction), social. Benefits maintained with continued exercise.

phases.goals: Build routine, overcome inertia, establish adherence
phases.criteria: Start achievable. Focus on consistency over intensity. Address barriers.

phases.goals: Reach therapeutic dose, optimize mood benefits
phases.criteria: Build to guideline levels. Expect mood improvements by 6-8 weeks.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise, prevent relapse
phases.criteria: Ongoing regular exercise. Benefits maintained with continued activity.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach with focus on adherence given motivational deficits in depression

absolute: Active suicidal intent (requires immediate crisis intervention)
relative: Severe depression (may need supervised or combined treatment)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Mood improvements typically within 6-10 weeks of regular exercise
outcomes.effect_sizes: Depression severity: SMD -0.62 to -0.82 (moderate to large effects). Comparable to antidepressants and psychotherapy.

key_findings: Exercise reduces depression - large effects
Comparable efficacy to antidepressants and psychotherapy
Both aerobic and resistance training effective
Higher intensity may be more effective
Benefits sustained with continued exercise
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Depression: SMD -0.62 (-0.81 to -0.42). Large clinically meaningful effects. Similar to first-line treatments.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from multiple Cochrane reviews and RCTs

references.citation: Cooney GM, et al. Exercise for depression. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2013
references.key_finding: Exercise reduces depression - moderate quality evidence

references.citation: Schuch FB, et al. Exercise as a treatment for depression: a meta-analysis adjusting for publication bias. J Psychiatr Res. 2016
references.key_finding: Large antidepressant effects of exercise when accounting for bias

references.citation: Kvam S, et al. Exercise as a treatment for depression: a meta-analysis. J Affect Disord. 2016
references.key_finding: Exercise effective treatment for depression

clinical_note: Exercise should be first-line treatment option alongside psychotherapy and medication. NOT 'just go for a walk' - needs to be structured, supervised program for best adherence. Initial inertia and low motivation major barriers - behavioral activation strategies essential. Supervised group programs may enhance adherence and provide social benefits. Exercise complements other treatments - combined approach often best. Screen for suicidality. Address sleep issues. Multidisciplinary care with psychologist/psychiatrist often appropriate. Exercise has no side effects and provides physical health benefits beyond mood improvement.

overview.pathophysiology: Chronic excessive worry and anxiety about various events/activities. Involves dysregulation of fear circuits, HPA axis, neurotransmitters (GABA, serotonin, norepinephrine).
overview.functional_impact: Excessive worry, restlessness, fatigue, concentration difficulties, irritability, muscle tension, sleep disturbance, functional impairment, reduced quality of life.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~3-6% of Australians. More common in females (2:1). Often comorbid with depression.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per anxiety disorder treatment guidelines and DSM-5

exercises.name: Moderate-Intensity Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Reduce anxiety symptoms - HIGHLY EFFECTIVE
exercises.modifications: Walking, jogging, cycling, swimming. Rhythmic repetitive activities beneficial.

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Reduce anxiety, improve physical and mental health
exercises.modifications: Progressive resistance training

exercises.name: Yoga
exercises.purpose: Reduce anxiety, improve relaxation and mindfulness
exercises.modifications: Hatha, vinyasa, restorative styles effective

exercises.name: Tai Chi / Qigong
exercises.purpose: Combine gentle movement, breathing, mindfulness
exercises.modifications: Well-suited for anxiety

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 8-12 weeks. Ongoing for sustained benefits.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise is highly effective treatment for anxiety disorders. Moderate to large effect sizes. Both acute (immediate post-exercise anxiety reduction) and chronic (long-term symptom reduction) benefits. Aerobic exercise most studied. Mind-body exercises particularly beneficial for anxiety. Mechanisms: reduced muscle tension, improved HPA axis regulation, neuroplasticity, self-efficacy, distraction. Effects sustained with continued exercise.

phases.goals: Build routine, manage initial anxiety about exercise, establish safety
phases.criteria: Start conservatively. Emphasize anxiety reduction benefits. Address concerns.

phases.goals: Build to therapeutic levels, optimize anxiety reduction
phases.criteria: Progress toward guidelines. Monitor anxiety response. Expect benefits by 8 weeks.
