phases.goals: Sustain exercise for long-term anxiety management
phases.criteria: Regular exercise 3-5x/week. Benefits maintained with continued activity.

progression.evidence_base: Gradual progression with emphasis on adherence and anxiety management

absolute: Severe panic attacks triggered by exercise
relative: Severe anxiety limiting participation (needs graded exposure)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Anxiety reduction within 4-8 weeks of regular exercise
outcomes.effect_sizes: Anxiety: SMD -0.48 to -1.0 (moderate to large effects). Comparable or superior to other interventions.

key_findings: Exercise reduces anxiety - large effects
Both aerobic and resistance training effective
Mind-body exercises (yoga, tai chi) particularly beneficial
Effects comparable to other first-line treatments
Acute and chronic benefits
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Anxiety: SMD -0.48 (-0.63 to -0.33) to large effects in some analyses. Clinically meaningful reductions.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence from multiple systematic reviews

references.citation: Aylett E, et al. Exercise in the treatment of clinical anxiety in general practice - a systematic review and meta-analysis. BMC Health Serv Res. 2018
references.key_finding: Exercise effective for treating anxiety in primary care

references.citation: Stubbs B, et al. An examination of the anxiolytic effects of exercise for people with anxiety and stress-related disorders: a meta-analysis. Psychiatry Res. 2017
references.key_finding: Large anxiolytic effects of exercise

references.citation: Cramer H, et al. Yoga for anxiety: a systematic review. Depress Anxiety. 2013
references.key_finding: Yoga reduces anxiety symptoms

clinical_note: Exercise should be considered first-line treatment alongside CBT. Particularly beneficial for GAD where worry and physical tension prominent. Some patients anxious about exercise itself - graded exposure and education important. Mind-body exercises may be particularly appealing for anxiety. Emphasize immediate post-exercise anxiety reduction (acute effects) to enhance motivation. Complements other treatments. Address comorbid depression if present. Regular routine and consistency important. Breathing techniques and relaxation can be incorporated. Multidisciplinary approach with psychologist often appropriate.

overview.pathophysiology: Chronic autoimmune demyelinating disease of CNS. Immune system attacks myelin sheath causing inflammation, demyelination, axonal damage. Results in varied neurological symptoms.
overview.functional_impact: Fatigue, weakness, spasticity, ataxia, balance problems, cognitive impairment, bladder dysfunction, pain, heat sensitivity, reduced mobility and independence.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~25,000 Australians. More common in females (3:1). Peak onset 20-40 years. Most common non-traumatic neurological disability in young adults.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per MS Australia and MSIF guidelines

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Reduce fatigue, improve fitness, mobility, mood - KEY INTERVENTION
exercises.modifications: Cycling, swimming, walking. Monitor for heat sensitivity. Short bouts if needed.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - reduces fatigue significantly

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Improve strength, function, combat muscle weakness
exercises.modifications: Major muscle groups. Adjust for weakness and fatigue.

exercises.name: Balance and Coordination Training
exercises.purpose: Reduce falls risk, improve stability and mobility
exercises.modifications: Task-specific, dual-task training, progressive challenge

exercises.name: Flexibility and Stretching
exercises.purpose: Manage spasticity, maintain ROM
exercises.modifications: Focus on commonly tight muscles. Gentle sustained stretches.

exercises.name: Aquatic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Exercise without heat sensitivity issues, buoyancy support
exercises.modifications: Cool pool temperature (<30°C). Various activities.

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Ongoing lifelong intervention
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise safe and highly beneficial in MS. Reduces fatigue (most significant outcome), improves fitness, strength, balance, mood, quality of life. Does NOT cause disease progression or relapses. Aquatic exercise particularly beneficial for heat-sensitive individuals. Progressive programs safe across all disability levels.

phases.goals: Establish tolerance, manage fatigue, build confidence
phases.criteria: Start conservatively. Short sessions. Monitor for overheating. Progress when consistently tolerating.

phases.goals: Build toward guideline levels, optimize benefits
phases.criteria: Progress duration and intensity gradually. Symptom improvements expected.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise, manage symptoms, maintain function
phases.criteria: Ongoing regular exercise 3-5x/week. Adapt during relapses.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach with fatigue management per MS exercise guidelines

relative: Significant heat sensitivity (use cooling, aquatic exercise)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Fatigue reduction within 6-8 weeks. Fitness improvements within 8-12 weeks.
key_outcomes: Improved mood and quality of life
outcomes.effect_sizes: Fatigue: SMD -0.53 (large effect). Walking: MD +50m on 6MWT. QoL: moderate to large improvements.

key_findings: Exercise reduces fatigue in MS (most significant benefit)
Improves fitness, strength, balance, mobility
Safe - does not cause relapses or progression
Benefits across all disability levels
Aquatic exercise particularly beneficial
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Fatigue: SMD -0.53 (-0.73 to -0.33). Mobility: SMD 0.19. Muscle power: SMD 0.15. QoL: significant improvements.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from multiple RCTs and Cochrane reviews

references.citation: Latimer-Cheung AE, et al. Effects of exercise training on fitness, mobility, fatigue, and health-related quality of life among adults with multiple sclerosis: a systematic review to inform guideline development. Arch Phys Med Rehabil. 2013
references.key_finding: Exercise training beneficial for fitness and fatigue in MS

references.citation: Motl RW, Sandroff BM. Benefits of exercise training in multiple sclerosis. Curr Neurol Neurosci Rep. 2015
references.key_finding: Comprehensive review of exercise benefits in MS

references.citation: MS Australia. Exercise and MS Guidelines. 2020
references.key_finding: Australian evidence-based exercise recommendations for MS

clinical_note: Fatigue is hallmark symptom and major target - exercise is most effective non-pharmaceutical intervention. Heat sensitivity common - use cooling strategies, aquatic exercise, climate control. Adapt program during relapses but encourage continued activity when able. Address bladder dysfunction before exercise. Screen for depression. Multidisciplinary approach essential. Exercise does NOT cause relapses or disease progression.

overview.pathophysiology: Inflammatory arthritis associated with psoriasis. Autoimmune condition affecting joints, entheses, skin. Characterized by joint inflammation, enthesitis, dactylitis, skin involvement.
overview.functional_impact: Joint pain, swelling, stiffness, enthesitis, dactylitis, fatigue, reduced ROM, functional limitations, skin manifestations.
overview.prevalence: Affects 6-42% of people with psoriasis. ~1% of Australian population. Peak onset 30-50 years.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per EULAR and GRAPPA PsA guidelines

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Reduce inflammation, improve cardiovascular health, manage fatigue
exercises.modifications: Low-impact preferred: cycling, swimming, walking. Adapt for joint involvement.

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 10-15 reps at moderate intensity, 2-3x/week
exercises.purpose: Improve strength, function, bone health
exercises.modifications: Avoid excessive loading on inflamed joints. Full ROM exercises.

exercises.name: Flexibility and Stretching
exercises.purpose: Maintain ROM, reduce stiffness
exercises.modifications: Gentle stretching, focus on commonly affected joints

exercises.name: Aquatic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Exercise with reduced joint loading
exercises.modifications: Warm water beneficial for stiffness

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Ongoing long-term management
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise safe and beneficial in PsA. Reduces pain, improves function, reduces cardiovascular risk (elevated in PsA). Does not cause disease flares. Multimodal programs combining aerobic, strength, flexibility most effective. Medications essential - exercise complements.

phases.goals: Establish tolerance, manage symptoms, build routine
phases.criteria: Start conservatively. Monitor for flares. Adapt for disease activity.

phases.goals: Build toward guideline levels, optimize benefits
phases.criteria: Progress intensity gradually. Expect improvements in function and symptoms.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise, manage disease long-term
phases.criteria: Regular exercise 3-5x/week. Adapt during flares.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach with flare management per PsA exercise guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvements in pain and function within 8-12 weeks
outcomes.effect_sizes: Moderate improvements in pain, function, and quality of life

references.citation: Roger-Silva D, et al. Physical exercise and psoriatic arthritis: a systematic review. Rheumatol Int. 2018
references.key_finding: Exercise beneficial for PsA symptoms and function

clinical_note: Address both joint and skin manifestations. Medications (DMARDs/biologics) essential - exercise complements. Elevated CVD risk - cardiovascular exercise important. Enthesitis common - avoid repetitive high-load entheseal stress. Adapt during flares but encourage continued activity. Multidisciplinary care with rheumatologist. Screen for depression/anxiety. Weight management important if obese.

overview.pathophysiology: Surgical replacement of hip joint with prosthesis. Post-operative rehabilitation essential for optimal outcomes. Tissue healing, muscle strengthening, and functional restoration required.
overview.functional_impact: Post-op: pain, limited ROM, reduced strength, altered gait, functional limitations. Goal: restore pain-free function and return to activities.
overview.prevalence: ~50,000 THRs performed annually in Australia. Common in older adults with hip OA. Success rate >95%.

outcome_measures: Hip Disability and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (HOOS)
assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per orthopaedic post-THR guidelines

exercises.name: Hip Strengthening
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 10-15 reps, daily initially then 3x/week
exercises.purpose: Restore hip muscle strength, especially abductors - CRITICAL
exercises.modifications: Progress from isometric to isotonic, then resistance. Respect precautions initially.

exercises.name: Gait Re-education
exercises.dosage: Daily practice, progress from frame to stick to independent
exercises.purpose: Normalize gait pattern, restore functional mobility
exercises.modifications: Progress weight-bearing as tolerated. Address Trendelenburg if present.

exercises.name: ROM Exercises
exercises.purpose: Restore hip ROM
exercises.modifications: Progress from passive to active. Hip precautions typically 6-12 weeks depending on approach.

exercises.name: Balance Training
exercises.purpose: Restore balance, reduce falls risk
exercises.modifications: Progress difficulty as strength improves

exercises.name: Cardiovascular Fitness
exercises.purpose: Restore fitness, general health
exercises.modifications: Cycling, walking. Avoid high-impact initially.

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Intensive rehabilitation 6-12 weeks. Continued strengthening 3-6 months. Maintenance ongoing.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Post-operative rehabilitation accelerates recovery and optimizes outcomes. Hip strengthening, especially abductors, critical for gait and function. Faster recovery with structured rehabilitation vs. no rehabilitation. Return to activities typically 3-6 months depending on activity type.

phases.goals: Mobilize safely, manage pain, prevent complications
phases.criteria: Walking with frame/crutches. Hip precautions strict. Gentle exercises.

phases.goals: Restore ROM, build strength, normalize gait
phases.criteria: Progress strengthening. Wean walking aids. Continue hip precautions.

phases.goals: Build strength, return to ADLs, prepare for higher function
phases.criteria: Progressive resistance training. Hip precautions relaxed (check with surgeon). Improve gait quality.

phases.goals: Return to desired activities, optimize strength
phases.criteria: Higher-level activities. Sport-specific training if appropriate.

phases.goals: Maintain strength, prevent contralateral hip issues
phases.criteria: Ongoing strengthening 2-3x/week.

progression.evidence_base: Phased approach per post-arthroplasty guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Independent mobility 6-12 weeks. Optimal function 3-6 months.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Large improvements in pain and function. Rehabilitation accelerates recovery.

key_findings: Structured rehabilitation accelerates recovery post-THR
Improves strength, function, gait
Earlier discharge with pre-hab and structured post-op rehab
Long-term outcomes excellent with or without formal PT but rehab accelerates
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Faster recovery and better short-term function with rehabilitation. Long-term outcomes similar.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate quality evidence for rehabilitation benefits

references.citation: Di Monaco M, et al. Rehabilitation after total hip arthroplasty: a systematic review of controlled trials. Eur J Phys Rehabil Med. 2009
references.key_finding: Exercise interventions accelerate functional recovery

references.citation: Australian Orthopaedic Association. Hip Replacement Clinical Pathway. 2020
references.key_finding: Australian best practice guidelines for THR rehabilitation

clinical_note: Hip precautions controversial - varies by surgical approach (posterior vs anterior). Check with surgeon. Posterior approach: avoid flexion >90°, adduction past midline, internal rotation for 6-12 weeks. Anterior approach: fewer/no precautions. DVT prophylaxis important. Monitor for dislocation risk. Address contralateral hip if symptomatic. Most patients do very well. Emphasize hip abductor strengthening - critical for gait.

overview.pathophysiology: Surgical replacement of knee joint with prosthesis. Post-operative rehabilitation essential for optimal ROM, strength, and functional outcomes.
overview.functional_impact: Post-op: pain, swelling, limited ROM (especially flexion), reduced strength, altered gait, functional limitations. Goal: restore pain-free function.
overview.prevalence: ~60,000 TKRs performed annually in Australia. Common in older adults with knee OA. Generally excellent outcomes.

outcome_measures: Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS)
assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per post-TKR rehabilitation guidelines

exercises.name: Knee ROM Exercises
exercises.purpose: Restore knee flexion and achieve full extension - CRITICAL early goal
exercises.modifications: Passive, active-assisted, then active. Heel slides, wall slides. Goal: 0° extension, 110-120° flexion.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - extension especially critical

exercises.name: Quadriceps Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Restore quadriceps strength - essential for function and gait
exercises.modifications: Quad sets, straight leg raises, progress to weight-bearing exercises.

exercises.name: Functional Exercises
exercises.purpose: Restore functional mobility
exercises.modifications: Sit-to-stand, step-ups, stairs, walking progression

exercises.name: Gait Re-education
exercises.purpose: Normalize gait, reduce compensations
exercises.modifications: Progress from frame to stick to independent. Address quad lag.

exercises.name: Balance Training
exercises.purpose: Restore balance, confidence
exercises.modifications: Progressive challenge

exercise_prescription.frequency: Multiple times daily initially for ROM, then 3-5x/week strengthening
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Intensive rehab 6-12 weeks. Continued strengthening 3-6 months. Ongoing maintenance.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Post-operative rehabilitation critical for optimal outcomes. Early focus on ROM (especially extension and flexion to 90°). Strengthening essential for function. Structured rehabilitation accelerates recovery and improves outcomes. Most achieve good-excellent results.

phases.goals: Control pain/swelling, restore ROM, early mobilization
phases.criteria: Begin ROM exercises day 1. Walking with frame. Ice, elevation. Goal: 90° flexion, full extension by week 1.

phases.goals: Restore ROM (goal 110-120° flexion), build strength, normalize gait
phases.criteria: Intensive ROM work. Progress strengthening. Wean walking aids. Address extension lag.

phases.goals: Optimize strength, return to ADLs
phases.criteria: Progressive resistance training. Functional activities. Normalized gait pattern.

phases.goals: Return to desired activities, maximize function
phases.criteria: Higher-level activities. Return to recreational activities.

phases.goals: Maintain strength and ROM
phases.criteria: Ongoing strengthening 2-3x/week. Stretching for ROM maintenance.

progression.evidence_base: Phased progressive approach per best practice TKR guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Independent mobility 6-12 weeks. Optimal function 3-6 months. Continued gains up to 1 year.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Large improvements in pain and function. Structured rehabilitation improves outcomes.

key_findings: Structured rehabilitation essential post-TKR
Improves ROM, strength, function
Early intensive rehabilitation beneficial
Telerehab emerging as alternative to in-person
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Rehabilitation accelerates recovery and optimizes outcomes. Effect sizes moderate to large.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence

references.citation: Artz N, et al. Effectiveness of physiotherapy exercise following total knee replacement: systematic review and meta-analysis. BMC Musculoskelet Disord. 2015
references.key_finding: Physiotherapy exercise effective post-TKR

references.citation: Australian Orthopaedic Association. Knee Replacement Clinical Pathway. 2020
references.key_finding: Australian best practice for TKR rehabilitation

clinical_note: Full extension CRITICAL - extension lag common problem affecting gait. Aggressive ROM work early. Cryotherapy helpful for swelling. CPM machines controversial - not essential. Home exercises critical - adherence major factor in outcomes. 10-20% not fully satisfied - often unrealistic expectations or persistent pain. Screen for complications (infection, loosening). Most do very well with structured rehab.

overview.pathophysiology: Atherosclerotic narrowing of coronary arteries reducing blood flow to myocardium. Can cause angina, MI, heart failure. Progressive disease with modifiable risk factors.
overview.functional_impact: Chest pain/angina, reduced exercise tolerance, fatigue, fear of exertion, functional limitations, high cardiovascular mortality risk.
overview.prevalence: Leading cause of death in Australia. Affects ~5% of adults, increasing with age. More common in males.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per cardiac rehabilitation guidelines and Heart Foundation

exercises.name: Moderate-Intensity Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Improve cardiovascular fitness, reduce symptoms, modify risk - CORNERSTONE
exercises.modifications: Walking, cycling most common. RPE 11-14. Monitor for angina.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - reduces mortality by 25%

exercises.name: High-Intensity Interval Training
exercises.dosage: 4x4 minutes at 85-95% peak HR with 3 min active recovery, 2-3x/week
exercises.purpose: Superior improvements in VO2peak vs continuous
exercises.modifications: Only for stable patients. Supervised initially. Emerging evidence.
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate to high quality evidence - safe in stable CAD

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Improve strength, muscle mass, complement aerobic benefits
exercises.modifications: Circuit training effective. Avoid valsalva.

exercises.name: Flexibility
exercises.purpose: Maintain mobility, warm-up/cool-down component
exercises.modifications: Gentle sustained stretches

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Cardiac rehabilitation 8-12 weeks. Ongoing lifelong exercise essential.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation reduces all-cause mortality by 25%, cardiac mortality by 26%. Improves exercise capacity, quality of life, risk factors. Reduces hospital admissions. Both moderate continuous and HIIT effective. Resistance training safe and beneficial. Effects require ongoing exercise.

phases.goals: Early mobilization post-event/procedure
phases.criteria: Supervised ambulation. Medical stability.

phases.goals: Restore fitness, education, risk modification
phases.criteria: Supervised exercise 2-3x/week. ECG monitoring if high risk. Gradual progression.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise, prevent events, manage risk factors
phases.criteria: Minimum 150 min/week moderate activity. Ongoing risk factor management.

progression.evidence_base: Phased cardiac rehabilitation per international guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Fitness improvements within 8-12 weeks. Mortality reduction long-term.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Mortality: RR 0.74 (0.64-0.86). VO2peak: MD +3.7 ml/kg/min. Large clinical benefits.

key_findings: Cardiac rehab reduces mortality by 25%
Improves exercise capacity and quality of life
Reduces hospital admissions
Cost-effective intervention
Both supervised and home-based programs effective
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: All-cause mortality: RR 0.74 (0.64-0.86). Cardiac mortality: RR 0.74 (0.63-0.87). Hospital admissions: RR 0.82 (0.70-0.96).
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and Cochrane reviews

references.citation: Anderson L, et al. Exercise-based cardiac rehabilitation for coronary heart disease. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2016
references.key_finding: Cardiac rehab reduces mortality and hospital admissions

references.citation: National Heart Foundation Australia. Cardiac Rehabilitation Guidelines. 2020
references.key_finding: Australian evidence-based cardiac rehab recommendations

references.citation: Balady GJ, et al. Core components of cardiac rehabilitation/secondary prevention programs. Circulation. 2007
references.key_finding: Comprehensive evidence-based cardiac rehab components

clinical_note: Cardiac rehabilitation underutilized despite strong evidence. Comprehensive risk factor modification essential (smoking cessation, BP, lipids, diabetes, weight). Medications crucial (antiplatelet, statins, ACE-I/ARB, beta-blockers). Psychosocial factors important - screen for depression/anxiety. Patient education on angina management and warning signs. Most patients can exercise safely with appropriate screening and monitoring. Multidisciplinary program ideal.

overview.pathophysiology: Degenerative joint disease of the knee involving cartilage breakdown, osteophyte formation, synovial inflammation, subchondral bone changes. Progressive loss of joint structure and function.
overview.functional_impact: Knee pain, stiffness, swelling, reduced ROM, difficulty with stairs/walking/squatting, altered gait, reduced physical activity, disability.
overview.prevalence: Most common form of arthritis. Affects ~10-15% of adults >60 years. Leading cause of disability in older adults. More common in females.

outcome_measures: Knee Injury and Osteoarthritis Outcome Score (KOOS)
assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per OARSI and RACGP knee OA guidelines

exercises.name: Quadriceps Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Improve knee strength, reduce pain, enhance function - KEY INTERVENTION
exercises.modifications: Include straight leg raises, leg press, knee extensions. Progress resistance.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - most important exercise component

exercises.name: Lower Limb Strengthening
exercises.purpose: Improve functional strength and mobility
exercises.modifications: Progress from assisted to unassisted, add resistance

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Improve fitness, weight management, reduce inflammation, pain modulation
exercises.modifications: Low-impact: walking, cycling, swimming, water aerobics

exercises.name: Balance and Proprioception
exercises.purpose: Improve stability, reduce falls risk
exercises.modifications: Single-leg stance, perturbations, unstable surfaces

exercises.name: Flexibility
exercises.purpose: Maintain ROM
exercises.modifications: Quadriceps, hamstrings, calf stretching
exercises.evidence_level: Low quality evidence alone, beneficial as part of program

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 8-12 weeks, ongoing long-term recommended
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise is first-line treatment for knee OA with moderate to large effects on pain and function. Effects similar to NSAIDs and paracetamol. Strengthening exercises, particularly quadriceps, most important component. Aquatic exercise highly beneficial. Weight loss enhances outcomes if overweight. Exercise delays/prevents need for joint replacement.

phases.goals: Pain control, basic strength, establish habit
phases.criteria: Low-moderate intensity. Progress when pain decreasing and strength improving.

phases.goals: Build strength and function
phases.criteria: Increase resistance and complexity. Progress aerobic duration.

phases.goals: Maintain gains, prevent progression
phases.criteria: Minimum 2-3x/week strengthening, regular aerobic activity.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach per OARSI guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvements in pain and function typically within 6-8 weeks
outcomes.effect_sizes: Pain: SMD -0.49 (95% CI -0.39 to -0.59). Function: SMD -0.52 (-0.39 to -0.64). Similar to NSAIDs.

key_findings: Exercise reduces pain and improves function in knee OA
Effects similar to medications (NSAIDs)
Land-based and aquatic exercise both effective
Strengthening particularly important
Weight loss enhances outcomes
Delays need for joint replacement
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Pain: SMD -0.49 (-0.39 to -0.59). Function: SMD -0.52 (-0.39 to -0.64). Quality of life: SMD 0.28.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and Cochrane reviews

references.citation: Fransen M, et al. Exercise for osteoarthritis of the knee. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015
references.key_finding: Exercise reduces pain and improves function in knee OA

references.citation: OARSI guidelines for the non-surgical management of knee, hip, and polyarticular osteoarthritis. Osteoarthritis Cartilage. 2019
references.key_finding: Exercise strongly recommended as core treatment for OA

clinical_note: Education critical - address fear of movement and misconceptions. Weight loss if BMI >25 (refer to APD). Consider bracing if significant instability. Intra-articular injections provide short-term relief but exercise essential long-term. Refer for joint replacement if conservative management fails and significant disability.

overview.pathophysiology: Chronic metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance and relative insulin deficiency. Results in hyperglycemia. Associated with obesity, physical inactivity, genetic factors.
overview.functional_impact: Often asymptomatic initially. Long-term: cardiovascular disease, neuropathy, nephropathy, retinopathy, foot ulcers, increased mortality. Fatigue, increased infection risk.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~5-6% of Australian adults, increasing to 15-20% in older adults. Rapidly increasing prevalence. Major public health burden.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per Diabetes Australia and RACGP guidelines

exercises.name: Moderate-Intensity Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Improve glycemic control, cardiovascular health, weight management
exercises.modifications: Walking most accessible. Variety important for adherence.

exercises.name: High-Intensity Interval Training (HIIT)
exercises.purpose: Superior glycemic control, time-efficient, cardiovascular benefits
exercises.modifications: 10:1-4:1 work:rest ratios effective
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - superior to continuous moderate

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training
exercises.purpose: Improve insulin sensitivity, muscle mass, strength, metabolic health
exercises.modifications: Major muscle groups. Circuit training effective.

exercises.name: Combined Aerobic + Resistance
exercises.purpose: Superior glycemic control compared to either alone
exercises.modifications: Can be same or separate sessions

exercise_prescription.frequency: 5-7 days per week activity (no more than 2 consecutive days without exercise), resistance 2-3x/week minimum
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Ongoing lifelong intervention
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise reduces HbA1c by 0.6-0.9% (clinically meaningful). Combined aerobic + resistance superior to either alone. Both moderate continuous and HIIT effective. Exercise improves insulin sensitivity, cardiovascular health, weight management. Reduces diabetes complications and mortality. Effects independent of weight loss.

phases.goals: Establish safe exercise routine, glucose monitoring
phases.criteria: Start conservatively, especially if sedentary. Monitor glucose pre/post initially. Educate on hypoglycemia.

phases.goals: Progress to recommended volumes, optimize glycemic control
phases.criteria: Build to 150-300 min/week aerobic + 2-3x/week resistance. HbA1c improvements expected.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise, maintain glycemic control, prevent complications
phases.criteria: Ongoing regular exercise. No more than 2 days without activity. Medication adjustments as needed.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach per Diabetes Australia exercise guidelines

absolute: Severe uncontrolled hyperglycemia (>15 mmol/L with ketones)
relative: Severe peripheral neuropathy (modify for foot protection)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: HbA1c reduction typically within 8-12 weeks. Continuous benefits with sustained exercise.
outcomes.effect_sizes: HbA1c: -0.67% (95% CI -0.84 to -0.49) with structured exercise. Combined aerobic+resistance: -0.9%. Effects additive with medications.

key_findings: Exercise reduces HbA1c by 0.6-0.9%
Combined aerobic + resistance superior to either alone
HIIT comparable or superior to moderate continuous
Effects independent of weight loss
Reduces cardiovascular events and mortality
Improves insulin sensitivity
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: HbA1c: -0.67% (-0.84 to -0.49). Combined training: -0.9%. Mortality: HR 0.65. Cardiovascular events: reduced by 20-30%.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and meta-analyses

references.citation: Umpierre D, et al. Physical activity advice only or structured exercise training and association with HbA1c levels in type 2 diabetes. JAMA. 2011
references.key_finding: Structured exercise reduces HbA1c by 0.67%, more than advice alone

references.citation: Colberg SR, et al. Exercise and type 2 diabetes: ACSM and ADA joint position statement. Diabetes Care. 2010
references.key_finding: Comprehensive evidence-based recommendations for exercise in diabetes

references.citation: Diabetes Australia. Physical Activity Guidelines. 2020
references.key_finding: Australian recommendations emphasizing combined aerobic and resistance training

clinical_note: Critical: Monitor glucose before/during/after exercise initially. Educate on hypoglycemia prevention and management. Carry fast-acting carbohydrate. May need medication adjustments as fitness improves - coordinate with GP/endocrinologist. Foot care essential - appropriate footwear, daily foot checks. Screen for complications. Address cardiovascular risk factors. Dietary intervention essential (refer to APD). Multidisciplinary approach critical.

overview.pathophysiology: Progressive lung disease characterized by persistent airflow limitation. Includes chronic bronchitis and emphysema. Caused primarily by smoking. Involves inflammation, mucus hypersecretion, airway remodeling, alveolar destruction.
overview.functional_impact: Dyspnea (breathlessness), reduced exercise tolerance, fatigue, frequent exacerbations, anxiety/depression, severe disability, reduced quality of life.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~7-8% of adults >40 years in Australia. Fourth leading cause of death. Major burden on health system.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per GOLD guidelines and Lung Foundation Australia

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Improve exercise capacity, reduce dyspnea, enhance quality of life
exercises.modifications: Walking, cycling. Interval training if continuous difficult. Use supplemental O2 if prescribed.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - cornerstone of pulmonary rehabilitation

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 8-12 reps, 2-3x/week, major muscle groups
exercises.purpose: Reverse muscle wasting, improve strength, functional capacity
exercises.modifications: Upper and lower limb exercises. Address peripheral muscle dysfunction.

exercises.name: Inspiratory Muscle Training
exercises.dosage: 30 breaths at 30-50% max inspiratory pressure, 5-7 days/week
exercises.purpose: Improve respiratory muscle strength and endurance, reduce dyspnea
exercises.modifications: Threshold IMT device. Progress resistance weekly.
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate to high quality evidence - beneficial adjunct

exercises.name: Upper Limb Training
exercises.purpose: Improve ADL capacity (dressing, grooming, etc)
exercises.modifications: Arm ergometry, resistance exercises, functional tasks

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 8 weeks for pulmonary rehabilitation. Ongoing maintenance essential.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Pulmonary rehabilitation (exercise + education) highly effective for COPD. Improves exercise capacity, quality of life, reduces dyspnea, anxiety, depression. Reduces hospital admissions by 50%. Effects maintained with ongoing exercise. Benefits across all COPD severities. Combined aerobic + resistance training recommended.

phases.goals: Comprehensive assessment, optimize medications, establish baseline
phases.criteria: Ensure clinically stable. No recent exacerbation. Medical clearance.

phases.goals: Improve exercise capacity, education, symptom management
phases.criteria: Progress intensity and duration as tolerated. Dyspnea management strategies.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise levels, prevent decline, reduce exacerbations
phases.criteria: Minimum 3x/week exercise. Community programs. Periodic reassessments.

progression.evidence_base: Minimum 8-week pulmonary rehabilitation per international guidelines

relative: SpO2 <88% at rest (may need supplemental O2)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvements in exercise capacity and QoL within 6-8 weeks
outcomes.effect_sizes: 6MWT: +44m (95% CI 32-56m). QoL: large improvements. Hospital admissions: RR 0.50 (0.38-0.65).

key_findings: Pulmonary rehabilitation highly effective for COPD
Improves exercise capacity, QoL, dyspnea
Reduces hospital admissions by 50%
Benefits across all COPD severities
Combined aerobic + resistance optimal
Maintenance exercise essential - benefits decline when stopping
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: 6MWT: MD +44m (32-56m). SGRQ: MD -7 units (large clinical benefit). Hospital admissions: RR 0.50 (0.38-0.65). Mortality: trend toward reduction.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs and Cochrane reviews

references.citation: McCarthy B, et al. Pulmonary rehabilitation for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2015
references.key_finding: Pulmonary rehab improves QoL, exercise capacity, reduces hospital admissions

references.citation: GOLD Guidelines. Global Strategy for Diagnosis, Management and Prevention of COPD. 2023
references.key_finding: Pulmonary rehabilitation recommended for all symptomatic COPD patients

references.citation: Lung Foundation Australia. Pulmonary Rehabilitation Toolkit. 2020
references.key_finding: Australian evidence-based pulmonary rehabilitation standards

clinical_note: Smoking cessation CRITICAL - refer to Quitline. Pulmonary rehabilitation ideally supervised, multidisciplinary (EP, PT, nurse, dietitian, psychologist). Teach dyspnea management strategies (pursed-lip breathing, pacing). Action plan for exacerbations essential. Nutritional support if underweight. Screen for anxiety/depression. Supplemental oxygen if indicated. Vaccination (influenza, pneumococcal) important. Benefits lost when exercise stops - ongoing maintenance essential.

overview.pathophysiology: Mental health disorder characterized by persistent low mood, anhedonia, cognitive changes. Involves dysregulation of neurotransmitters (serotonin, norepinephrine, dopamine), HPA axis dysfunction, neuroinflammation, reduced neuroplasticity.
overview.functional_impact: Persistent sadness, loss of interest/pleasure, fatigue, cognitive impairment, sleep disturbance, appetite changes, reduced functioning, suicidal ideation, severe disability.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~6% of Australians in any year. Lifetime prevalence ~15-20%. Leading cause of disability worldwide.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per ESSA Mental Health Position Statement and clinical practice guidelines

exercises.name: Moderate-Intensity Aerobic Exercise
exercises.purpose: Reduce depressive symptoms, improve mood, neuroplasticity
exercises.modifications: Walking, jogging, cycling, swimming. Outdoor exercise may provide additional benefits. Patient preference important.
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - comparable to antidepressants/psychotherapy

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 8-12 reps, 2-3x/week, major muscle groups
exercises.purpose: Reduce depression, improve self-efficacy, physical health
exercises.modifications: Can be standalone or combined with aerobic

exercises.name: Yoga
exercises.purpose: Reduce depressive symptoms, mindfulness, stress management
exercises.modifications: Various styles effective. Include breathing and meditation components.

exercises.name: Group Exercise Classes
exercises.purpose: Combine exercise benefits with social interaction
exercises.modifications: Any group format - social component important
exercises.evidence_level: Moderate quality evidence - social benefits enhance outcomes

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Minimum 8-12 weeks for clinically significant improvement. Ongoing exercise for sustained benefits.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise has large antidepressant effects, comparable to medications and psychotherapy. Effects evident after 4-8 weeks. Both aerobic and resistance training effective. Supervised programs more effective than unsupervised. Higher doses associated with greater effects. Mechanisms include altered neurotransmitters, increased BDNF, improved sleep, enhanced self-efficacy, social interaction.

phases.goals: Establish exercise habit, overcome barriers, build confidence
phases.criteria: Start at comfortable level. Focus on positive experience and consistency over intensity.

phases.goals: Build to recommended volumes, optimize antidepressant effects
phases.criteria: Gradually progress to guideline levels. Symptom reduction expected by 6-8 weeks.

phases.goals: Sustain exercise, maintain mood stability, prevent relapse
phases.criteria: Ongoing regular exercise. Self-management capability. Exercise as relapse prevention tool.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive approach addressing depression-specific barriers (amotivation, fatigue)

absolute: Acute suicidal ideation requiring immediate psychiatric care

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Clinically significant reduction in depressive symptoms within 6-12 weeks
outcomes.effect_sizes: Depression severity: SMD -1.03 (95% CI -1.42 to -0.63) for supervised programs. Effects comparable to antidepressants.

key_findings: Exercise reduces depressive symptoms with large effects
Effects comparable to antidepressants and psychotherapy
Both aerobic and resistance training effective
Supervised programs superior to unsupervised
Higher doses associated with greater effects
Benefits for both clinical depression and subthreshold symptoms
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Depression severity: SMD -1.03 (-1.42 to -0.63) supervised programs. SMD -0.62 overall. Response/remission rates comparable to medications.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence from numerous RCTs and meta-analyses

references.citation: Schuch FB, et al. Exercise as a treatment for depression: a meta-analysis. J Psychiatr Res. 2016
references.key_finding: Exercise reduces depression with large effect sizes

references.citation: Cooney GM, et al. Exercise for depression. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2013
references.key_finding: Exercise effective for reducing symptoms of depression

references.citation: ESSA Position Statement: Exercise and Mental Health. 2021
references.key_finding: Exercise Physiologists play key role in depression management

clinical_note: Exercise adjunct to, not replacement for, treatment in moderate-severe depression. Collaborate with psychologist/psychiatrist. Address barriers specific to depression (amotivation, fatigue, anhedonia). Start where patient is - 'something rather than nothing'. Group exercise beneficial for social connection. Monitor for suicidal ideation. May take time to see benefits - patience and persistence important. Exercise also effective for relapse prevention.

overview.pathophysiology: Brain injury due to interrupted blood supply (ischemic) or bleeding (hemorrhagic). Results in focal neurological deficits depending on location - motor, sensory, cognitive, language impairments.
overview.functional_impact: Hemiparesis/hemiplegia, impaired balance and gait, reduced fitness, spasticity, cognitive impairment, aphasia, reduced independence, high falls risk, depression.
overview.prevalence: ~50,000 strokes annually in Australia. Leading cause of adult disability. Survival rates improving - focus on recovery and prevention of recurrence.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per Stroke Foundation Clinical Guidelines

exercises.name: Task-Specific Gait Training
exercises.purpose: Improve walking speed, endurance, quality - KEY INTERVENTION
exercises.modifications: Treadmill training, overground walking, harness support if needed
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - cornerstone of stroke rehab

exercises.name: Cardiovascular Fitness Training
exercises.purpose: Improve fitness, reduce cardiovascular risk, enhance endurance
exercises.modifications: Cycling, walking, combined upper/lower limb ergometry

exercises.name: Progressive Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 8-12 reps, 2-3x/week, affected and unaffected limbs
exercises.purpose: Improve strength, muscle mass, function
exercises.modifications: Include paretic limb - strength training does NOT increase spasticity
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - safe and effective

exercises.name: Balance and Mobility Training
exercises.purpose: Reduce falls risk, improve stability, confidence
exercises.modifications: Standing balance, weight shifting, reaching, obstacle navigation

exercises.name: Upper Limb Training
exercises.dosage: High repetition practice, daily if possible
exercises.purpose: Improve arm function, ADL independence
exercises.modifications: Constraint-induced movement therapy, repetitive task practice

exercise_prescription.frequency: 5-7 days per week ideally - high intensity and dose important
exercise_prescription.program_duration: Intensive rehabilitation first 6 months. Ongoing exercise for continued gains and secondary prevention.
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise improves motor recovery, walking, balance, fitness, independence. High-intensity, task-specific training most effective. Earlier intervention better but gains possible even years post-stroke. Cardiovascular fitness training essential for secondary prevention. Resistance training safe and does not worsen spasticity.

phases.goals: Early mobilization, prevent complications
phases.criteria: Medical stability. Early sitting/standing. Progress as tolerated.

phases.goals: Maximize recovery, intensive task-specific training
phases.criteria: High-intensity rehabilitation. Progress walking, strength, function. This is the critical recovery window.

phases.goals: Continue improvements, maintain gains, secondary prevention
phases.criteria: Ongoing exercise 3-5x/week. Further gains still possible. Focus also on fitness for secondary prevention.

progression.evidence_base: Intensive early rehabilitation within first 6 months critical per Stroke Foundation guidelines

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Greatest gains first 3-6 months. Continued improvements possible for years.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Gait speed: MD +0.13 m/s. Walking distance: MD +40-50m. Balance: SMD 0.37. Cardiovascular fitness: 10-30% improvement.

key_findings: Exercise improves motor recovery, gait, balance, fitness post-stroke
Task-specific training most effective
Cardiovascular fitness training improves endurance and secondary prevention
Resistance training safe and beneficial
High-intensity training superior to low
Earlier intervention better but gains possible in chronic phase
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Gait speed: MD +0.13 m/s (0.09-0.17). Walking distance: MD +40-50m. VO2peak: MD +2.4 ml/kg/min. Balance: SMD 0.37.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence from numerous RCTs and systematic reviews

references.citation: Saunders DH, et al. Physical fitness training for stroke patients. Cochrane Database Syst Rev. 2016
references.key_finding: Fitness training improves walking, balance, and fitness post-stroke

references.citation: Stroke Foundation. Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management. 2023
references.key_finding: Comprehensive Australian evidence-based stroke rehabilitation guidelines

references.citation: Veerbeek JM, et al. What is the evidence for physical therapy poststroke? Neurorehabil Neural Repair. 2014
references.key_finding: Strong evidence for task-specific training and fitness interventions

clinical_note: Intensive early rehabilitation critical - first 6 months is key recovery window. Cardiovascular fitness training often neglected but essential for secondary prevention (50% recurrence risk). Address multiple risk factors (BP, lipids, diabetes, smoking, AF). Screen for depression (common). Constraint-induced movement therapy for upper limb. High repetition, task-specific training key principles. Multidisciplinary team essential (PT, OT, SLP, EP, psychologist).

overview.pathophysiology: Degenerative condition of plantar fascia, not primarily inflammatory. Involves collagen breakdown, neovascularization. Often from overload/overuse.
overview.functional_impact: Heel pain worst with first steps in morning, prolonged standing/walking, reduced walking tolerance, altered gait, activity limitation.
overview.prevalence: Affects ~10% of population at some point. Common in runners, overweight individuals, occupations requiring prolonged standing.

outcome_measures: Pain VAS (first step, end of day)
assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per best practice clinical guidelines for plantar heel pain

exercises.name: Plantar Fascia Specific Stretching
exercises.purpose: Plantar fascia loading and stretching - FIRST-LINE
exercises.modifications: Seated, toes extended, ankle dorsiflexed
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - superior to calf stretching alone

exercises.name: High-Load Strength Training
exercises.dosage: Single-leg calf raises off step edge, 3 sets to fatigue, every other day
exercises.purpose: Load plantar fascia and Achilles, promote tissue remodeling
exercises.modifications: Slow eccentric phase emphasis
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - most effective intervention

exercises.name: Calf Stretching
exercises.purpose: Address limited ankle dorsiflexion
exercises.modifications: Both gastrocnemius (knee straight) and soleus (knee bent)

exercises.name: Foot Intrinsic Strengthening
exercises.dosage: Towel curls, toe spreading, 2-3 sets, daily
exercises.purpose: Improve foot muscle support
exercises.modifications: Short foot exercise

exercise_prescription.frequency: Daily stretching, strength training every other day
exercise_prescription.program_duration: 8-12 weeks typically, some require longer
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: High-load strength training most effective long-term intervention (superior at 12 months). Plantar fascia stretching effective short-term. Combination recommended. Orthoses provide short-term symptom relief. Corticosteroid injections provide short-term relief but no long-term benefit and potential for tissue damage.

phases.goals: Reduce acute pain, activity modification
phases.criteria: Stretching, ice, load management. Progress when pain decreasing.

phases.goals: Progressive loading of plantar fascia
phases.criteria: High-load strength training. Some pain (<5/10) acceptable during exercise.

phases.goals: Gradual return to full activities
phases.criteria: Progressive return to running/sport. Continued strengthening.

phases.goals: Prevent recurrence
phases.criteria: Continued calf/foot strengthening 2-3x/week.

progression.evidence_base: Progressive loading approach per best evidence

relative: Severe acute pain (may need initial rest)
red_flags: Bilateral heel pain with systemic symptoms

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Improvement within 6-12 weeks. Complete resolution may take 6-18 months.
outcomes.effect_sizes: High-load strength training: Large effect sizes (d>1.0) at 12 months. Plantar fascia stretching: Moderate effects short-term.

key_findings: High-load strength training most effective long-term
Plantar fascia stretching effective short-term
Orthoses provide short-term symptom relief
Corticosteroid injections: short-term relief only, potential harm
Shockwave therapy: moderate evidence
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: High-load training superior to other interventions at 12 months. Plantar fascia stretch superior to calf stretch at 8 weeks.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: Moderate to high quality evidence for exercise interventions

references.citation: Rathleff MS, et al. High-load strength training improves outcome in patients with plantar fasciitis: a randomized controlled trial. Scand J Med Sci Sports. 2015
references.key_finding: High-load strength training superior to plantar fascia stretching at 3 months

references.citation: Digiovanni BF, et al. Plantar fascia-specific stretching exercise improves outcomes in patients with chronic plantar fasciitis. J Bone Joint Surg Am. 2006
references.key_finding: Plantar fascia stretching superior to Achilles stretching

clinical_note: Address contributing factors: footwear, training load, weight, foot biomechanics. Consider orthoses for short-term symptom relief but exercise is key long-term. Avoid corticosteroid injections (tissue damage risk). Night splints may help in recalcitrant cases. Most resolve with conservative management. Refer for imaging if not improving by 6-12 weeks to rule out other pathology.

overview.pathophysiology: Malignant tumor of breast tissue. Treatment involves surgery, chemotherapy, radiation, hormone therapy. Side effects include fatigue, lymphedema risk, reduced fitness, muscle loss, bone loss, neuropathy.
overview.functional_impact: Treatment-related fatigue, reduced fitness, muscle weakness, lymphedema (if nodes removed), reduced shoulder ROM, bone loss, neuropathy, cognitive changes, psychosocial impact.
overview.prevalence: Most common cancer in Australian women (1 in 7 lifetime risk). Survival rates >90% at 5 years with early detection.

assessment.evidence_base: Assessment per Clinical Oncology Society of Australia exercise guidelines

exercises.name: Aerobic Exercise
exercises.dosage: 150 minutes/week moderate intensity OR 75 minutes vigorous
exercises.purpose: Reduce fatigue, improve fitness, QoL, potentially reduce recurrence risk
exercises.modifications: Walking most common. Build up gradually during treatment.

exercises.name: Resistance Training
exercises.dosage: 2-3 sets of 8-12 reps, 2-3x/week, major muscle groups
exercises.purpose: Prevent muscle loss, improve strength, bone health, lymphedema safe
exercises.modifications: Include affected arm - lymphedema not contraindication with gradual progression
exercises.evidence_level: High quality evidence - safe including for lymphedema risk

exercises.name: Upper Body Exercise
exercises.purpose: Restore shoulder function, prevent lymphedema
exercises.modifications: Post-surgery: progress ROM as healing allows. Strengthening when cleared.

exercises.name: Flexibility
exercises.purpose: Maintain mobility, relaxation
exercises.modifications: Include shoulder/chest stretching

exercise_prescription.program_duration: Ongoing during and after treatment
exercise_prescription.evidence_summary: Exercise safe and beneficial during and after breast cancer treatment. Reduces fatigue, improves QoL, physical function. Does NOT increase lymphedema risk - progressive resistance training safe. May reduce recurrence risk and improve survival. Supervised programs more effective than advice alone.

phases.goals: Maintain function, manage fatigue, prevent deconditioning
phases.criteria: Modify intensity for treatment side effects. Aim for 'something rather than nothing'.

phases.goals: Restore fitness, build toward guidelines
phases.criteria: Gradual progression toward 150 min/week aerobic + 2x/week resistance.

phases.goals: Meet/exceed guidelines, optimize health, reduce recurrence
phases.criteria: Sustained physical activity. Higher volumes may provide greater benefits.

progression.evidence_base: Exercise safe during treatment per multiple guidelines (COSA, ACSM)

outcomes.expected_timeframe: Fatigue improvements within 4-8 weeks. Fitness improvements within 8-12 weeks.
outcomes.effect_sizes: Fatigue: SMD -0.38 to -0.82. QoL: SMD 0.48. Physical function: SMD 0.42. Recurrence: HR ~0.80.

key_findings: Exercise reduces cancer-related fatigue
Improves QoL and physical function
Safe during and after treatment
Does not increase lymphedema risk
May reduce recurrence by 15-20%
May improve survival
meta_analysis_summary.pooled_effects: Fatigue: SMD -0.82 (-1.4 to -0.24) post-intervention. QoL: SMD 0.48. Recurrence: HR 0.76-0.82. Mortality: HR 0.67-0.73.
meta_analysis_summary.quality_of_evidence: High quality evidence from extensive RCTs for symptom management. Moderate quality for recurrence/survival.

references.citation: Speck RM, et al. An update of controlled physical activity trials in cancer survivors: a systematic review and meta-analysis. J Cancer Surviv. 2010
references.key_finding: Exercise improves QoL, physical function, and reduces fatigue in cancer survivors

references.citation: Clinical Oncology Society of Australia. Exercise in Cancer Care. 2018
references.key_finding: Exercise should be embedded in standard cancer care

references.citation: Schmitz KH, et al. Weight lifting in women with breast-cancer-related lymphedema. N Engl J Med. 2009
references.key_finding: Progressive resistance training safe and does not worsen lymphedema

clinical_note: Exercise is medicine in cancer care. Safe during treatment with modifications. Progressive resistance training does NOT increase lymphedema risk - this is outdated advice. Supervised programs ideal but home-based also beneficial. Address barriers (fatigue, time, motivation). Coordinate with oncology team. Screen for bone metastases before high-impact exercise. Multidisciplinary approach essential.
